hypothesis: To these he committed me, commending me to their care and saying, this man is a stranger, so take him with you and teach him how to gather that he may get his daily bread, and you will earn your reward and recompense in heaven.

reference: NOW SLEEPING UNDER THESE TREES WERE MANY APES WHICH WHEN THEY SAW US ROSE AND FLED FROM US AND SWARMED UP AMONG THE BRANCHES WHEREUPON MY COMPANIONS BEGAN TO PELT THEM WITH WHAT THEY HAD IN THEIR BAGS AND THE APES FELL TO PLUCKING OF THE FRUIT OF THE TREES AND CASTING THEM AT THE FOLK
hypothesis: Now sleeping under these trees were many apes, which, when they saw us, rose and fled from us and swarmed up among the branches. whereupon my companions began to pelt them with what they had in their bags, and the apes fell to plucking of the fruit of the trees and casting them at the folk.

reference: WE WEIGHED ANCHOR AND SHAHRAZAD PERCEIVED THE DAWN OF DAY AND CEASED SAYING HER PERMITTED SAY
hypothesis: We weighed anchor and Shahrazad perceived the dawn of the day and ceased saying her permitted say.

reference: WHEN IT WAS THE FIVE HUNDRED AND FIFTY NINTH NIGHT
hypothesis: When it was the 559th night.

reference: AND CEASED NOT SAILING TILL WE ARRIVED SAFELY AT BASSORAH
hypothesis: And ceased not sailing till we arrived safely at Basra.

reference: THERE I ABODE A LITTLE AND THEN WENT ON TO BAGHDAD WHERE I ENTERED MY QUARTER AND FOUND MY HOUSE AND FOREGATHERED WITH MY FAMILY AND SALUTED MY FRIENDS WHO GAVE ME JOY OF MY SAFE RETURN AND I LAID UP ALL MY GOODS AND VALUABLES IN MY STOREHOUSES
hypothesis: There I abode a little and then went on to Baghdad where I entered my quarter and found my house and were gathered with my family and saluted my friends who gave me joy of my safe return.

reference: AFTER WHICH I RETURNED TO MY OLD MERRY WAY OF LIFE AND FORGOT ALL I HAD SUFFERED IN THE GREAT PROFIT AND GAIN I HAD MADE
hypothesis: After which I returned to my old merry way of life and forgot all I had suffered in the great profit and gain I had made.

reference: NEXT MORNING AS SOON AS IT WAS LIGHT HE PRAYED THE DAWN PRAYER AND AFTER BLESSING MOHAMMED THE CREAM OF ALL CREATURES BETOOK HIMSELF TO THE HOUSE OF SINDBAD THE SEAMAN AND WISHED HIM A GOOD DAY
hypothesis: Next morning as soon as it was light, he prayed the dawn prayer, and after blessing Muhammad, the cream of all creatures, betook himself to the house of Sinbad the Seaman, and wished him a good day.

reference: HERE I FOUND A GREAT SHIP READY FOR SEA AND FULL OF MERCHANTS AND NOTABLES WHO HAD WITH THEM GOODS OF PRICE SO I EMBARKED MY BALES THEREIN
hypothesis: Here I found a great ship ready for sea and full of merchants and notables who had with them goods of price.

reference: HAPLY AMONGST YOU IS ONE RIGHTEOUS WHOSE PRAYERS THE LORD WILL ACCEPT
hypothesis: Happily amongst you is one righteous whose prayers the Lord will accept.

reference: PRESENTLY THE SHIP STRUCK THE MOUNTAIN AND BROKE UP AND ALL AND EVERYTHING ON BOARD OF HER WERE PLUNGED INTO THE SEA
hypothesis: Presently the ship struck the mountain and broke up and all and everything on board of her were plunged into the sea.

reference: BUT IT BURNETH IN THEIR BELLIES SO THEY CAST IT UP AGAIN AND IT CONGEALETH ON THE SURFACE OF THE WATER WHEREBY ITS COLOR AND QUANTITIES ARE CHANGED AND AT LAST THE WAVES CAST IT ASHORE AND THE TRAVELLERS AND MERCHANTS WHO KNOW IT COLLECT IT AND SELL IT
hypothesis: But it burneth in their bellies. so they cast it up again and it congee on the surface of the water, whereby its colour and quantities are changed. And at last the waves cast it ashore and the travellers and merchants who know it collect it and sell it.

reference: EACH THAT DIED WE WASHED AND SHROUDED IN SOME OF THE CLOTHES AND LINEN CAST ASHORE BY THE TIDES AND AFTER A LITTLE THE REST OF MY FELLOWS PERISHED ONE BY ONE TILL I HAD BURIED THE LAST OF THE PARTY AND ABODE ALONE ON THE ISLAND WITH BUT A LITTLE PROVISION LEFT I WHO WAS WONT TO HAVE SO MUCH
hypothesis: Each that died we washed and shrouded in some of the clothes and linen cast ashore by the tides.

reference: BUT THERE IS MAJESTY AND THERE IS NO MIGHT SAVE IN ALLAH THE GLORIOUS THE GREAT
hypothesis: But there is majesty and there is no might save in Allah, the Glorious, the Great.

hypothesis: When it was the 561st night.

reference: THEN SIGHING FOR MYSELF I SET TO WORK COLLECTING A NUMBER OF PIECES OF CHINESE AND COMORIN ALOES WOOD AND I BOUND THEM TOGETHER WITH ROPES FROM THE WRECKAGE THEN I CHOSE OUT FROM THE BROKEN UP SHIPS STRAIGHT PLANKS OF EVEN SIZE AND FIXED THEM FIRMLY UPON THE ALOES WOOD MAKING ME A BOAT RAFT A LITTLE NARROWER THAN THE CHANNEL OF THE STREAM AND I TIED IT TIGHTLY AND FIRMLY AS THOUGH IT WERE NAILED
hypothesis: Then

reference: LAND AFTER LAND SHALT THOU SEEK AND FIND BUT NO OTHER LIFE ON THY WISH SHALL WAIT FRET NOT THY SOUL IN THY THOUGHTS O NIGHT ALL WOES SHALL END OR SOONER OR LATE
hypothesis: Land after land shalt thou see confined.

reference: I ROWED MY CONVEYANCE INTO THE PLACE WHICH WAS INTENSELY DARK AND THE CURRENT CARRIED THE RAFT WITH IT DOWN THE UNDERGROUND CHANNEL
hypothesis: I rode my conveyance into the place, which was intensely dark, and the current carried the raft with it down the underground channel.

reference: AND I THREW MYSELF DOWN UPON MY FACE ON THE RAFT BY REASON OF THE NARROWNESS OF THE CHANNEL WHILST THE STREAM CEASED NOT TO CARRY ME ALONG KNOWING NOT NIGHT FROM DAY FOR THE EXCESS OF THE GLOOM WHICH ENCOMPASSED ME ABOUT AND MY TERROR AND CONCERN FOR MYSELF LEST I SHOULD PERISH
hypothesis: And I threw myself down upon my face on the raft by reason of the narrowness of the channel, whilst the stream ceased not to carry me along, knowing not night from day for the excess of the gloom which encompassed me about in my terror and concern for myself lest I should perish.

reference: WHEN I AWOKE AT LAST I FOUND MYSELF IN THE LIGHT OF HEAVEN AND OPENING MY EYES I SAW MYSELF IN A BROAD STREAM AND THE RAFT MOORED TO AN ISLAND IN THE MIDST OF A NUMBER OF INDIANS AND ABYSSINIANS
hypothesis: When I awoke at last, I found myself in the light of heaven, and opening my eyes I saw myself in a broad stream and the raft moored to an island in the midst of a number of Indians and Abyssinians.

reference: BUT I WAS DELIGHTED AT MY ESCAPE FROM THE RIVER
hypothesis: But I was delighted at my escape from the river.

reference: WHEN THEY SAW I UNDERSTOOD THEM NOT AND MADE THEM NO ANSWER ONE OF THEM CAME FORWARD AND SAID TO ME IN ARABIC PEACE BE WITH THEE O MY BROTHER
hypothesis: When they saw I understood them not and made them no answer, one of them came forward and said to me in Arabic.

reference: O MY BROTHER ANSWERED HE WE ARE HUSBANDMEN AND TILLERS OF THE SOIL WHO CAME OUT TO WATER OUR FIELDS AND PLANTATIONS AND FINDING THEE ASLEEP ON THIS RAFT LAID HOLD OF IT AND MADE IT FAST BY US AGAINST THOU SHOULDST AWAKE AT THY LEISURE
hypothesis: O my brother, answered he, we are husbandmen and tillers of the soil, who came out to water our fields and plantations and finding thee asleep on this raft, laid hold of it and made it fast by us against thou shouldest awake at thy leisure.

reference: I ANSWERED FOR ALLAH'S SAKE O MY LORD ERE I SPEAK GIVE ME SOMEWHAT TO EAT FOR I AM STARVING AND AFTER ASK ME WHAT THOU WILT
hypothesis: I answered, for Allah's sake, O my lord, ere I speak, give me somewhat to eat, for I am starving. and after ask me what thou wilt.

reference: WHEN IT WAS THE FIVE HUNDRED AND SIXTY SECOND NIGHT
hypothesis: When it was the 562nd night.

reference: SHE SAID IT HATH REACHED ME O AUSPICIOUS KING THAT SINDBAD THE SEAMAN CONTINUED WHEN I LANDED AND FOUND MYSELF AMONGST THE INDIANS AND ABYSSINIANS AND HAD TAKEN SOME REST THEY CONSULTED AMONG THEMSELVES AND SAID TO ONE ANOTHER THERE IS NO HELP FOR IT BUT WE CARRY HIM WITH US AND PRESENT HIM TO OUR KING THAT HE MAY ACQUAINT HIM WITH HIS ADVENTURES
hypothesis: She said, It hath reached me, O auspicious King, that Sindbad the Seaman continued.

reference: SO I CONSORTED WITH THE CHIEF OF THE ISLANDERS AND THEY PAID ME THE UTMOST RESPECT
hypothesis: So I consorted with the chief of the islanders and they paid me the utmost respect.

reference: SO I ROSE WITHOUT STAY OR DELAY AND KISSED THE KING'S HAND AND ACQUAINTED HIM WITH MY LONGING TO SET OUT WITH THE MERCHANTS FOR THAT I PINED AFTER MY PEOPLE AND MINE OWN LAND
hypothesis: So I rose without stay or delay and kissed the king's hand and acquainted him with my longing to set out with the merchants.

reference: QUOTH HE THOU ART THINE OWN MASTER YET IF IT BE THY WILL TO ABIDE WITH US ON OUR HEAD AND EYES BE IT FOR THOU GLADDENEST US WITH THY COMPANY
hypothesis: Quoth he, thou art thine own master yet if it be thy will to abide with us, on our head and eyes be it, for thou gladden us with thy company.

hypothesis: By Allah, O my lord, answered I.

reference: THEN I TOOK LEAVE OF HIM AND OF ALL MY INTIMATES AND ACQUAINTANCES IN THE ISLAND AND EMBARKED WITH THE MERCHANTS AFORESAID
hypothesis: Then I took leave of him and of all my intimates and acquaintances in the island, and embarked with the merchants aforesaid.

reference: HE ASKED ME WHENCE THEY CAME AND I SAID TO HIM BY ALLAH O COMMANDER OF THE FAITHFUL I KNOW NOT THE NAME OF THE CITY NOR THE WAY THITHER
hypothesis: He asked me whence they came and I said to him, By Allah, O Commander of the Faithful, I know not the name of the city nor the way thither.

reference: FOR STATE PROCESSIONS A THRONE IS SET FOR HIM UPON A HUGE ELEPHANT ELEVEN CUBITS HIGH AND UPON THIS HE SITTETH HAVING HIS GREAT LORDS AND OFFICERS AND GUESTS STANDING IN TWO RANKS ON HIS RIGHT HAND AND ON HIS LEFT
hypothesis: For state processions, a throne is set for him upon a huge elephant eleven cubits high, and upon this he sitteth, having his great lords and officers and guests standing in two ranks, on his right hand and on his left.

reference: HIS LETTER HATH SHOWN ME THIS AND AS FOR THE MIGHTINESS OF HIS DOMINION THOU HAST TOLD US WHAT THOU HAST EYE WITNESSED
hypothesis: His letter hath shown me this. And as for the mightiness of his dominion, thou hast told us what thou hast eye witnessed.

reference: PRESENTLY MY FRIENDS CAME TO ME AND I DISTRIBUTED PRESENTS AMONG MY FAMILY AND GAVE ALMS AND LARGESSE AFTER WHICH I YIELDED MYSELF TO JOYANCE AND ENJOYMENT MIRTH AND MERRY MAKING AND FORGOT ALL THAT I HAD SUFFERED
hypothesis: Presently my friends came to me and I distributed presents among my family and gave alms and largesse, after which I yielded myself to joyance and enjoyment, mirth and merrymaking, and forgot all that I had suffered.

reference: SUCH THEN O MY BROTHERS IS THE HISTORY OF WHAT BEFEL ME IN MY SIXTH VOYAGE AND TO MORROW INSHALLAH
hypothesis: Such then, O my brothers, is the history of what befell me in my 6th voyage and tomorrow, Inshallah.

reference: I WILL TELL YOU THE STORY OF MY SEVENTH AND LAST VOYAGE WHICH IS STILL MORE WONDROUS AND MARVELLOUS THAN THAT OF THE FIRST SIX
hypothesis: I will tell you the story of my 7th and last voyage, which is still more wondrous and marvellous than that of the 1st six.

reference: WHEN IT WAS THE FIVE HUNDRED AND SIXTY THIRD NIGHT
hypothesis: When it was the 563rd night.

hypothesis: She said, It hath reached me, O auspicious King, that when Sinbad the Seaman had related the history of what befell him in his 6th voyage, and all the company had dispersed, Sinbad the Landsman went home and slept as of wont.

reference: THE SEVENTH VOYAGE OF SINDBAD THE SEAMAN
hypothesis: The 7th Voyage of Sinbad the Seaman.

reference: KNOW O COMPANY THAT AFTER MY RETURN FROM MY SIXTH VOYAGE WHICH BROUGHT ME ABUNDANT PROFIT I RESUMED MY FORMER LIFE IN ALL POSSIBLE JOYANCE AND ENJOYMENT AND MIRTH AND MAKING MERRY DAY AND NIGHT AND I TARRIED SOME TIME IN THIS SOLACE AND SATISFACTION TILL MY SOUL BEGAN ONCE MORE TO LONG TO SAIL THE SEAS AND SEE FOREIGN COUNTRIES AND COMPANY WITH MERCHANTS AND HEAR NEW THINGS
hypothesis: Know, O company, that after my return from my 6th voyage, which brought me abundant profit, I resumed my former life and all possible joyance and enjoyment and mirth and making merry day and night.

reference: SO HAVING MADE UP MY MIND I PACKED UP IN BALES A QUANTITY OF PRECIOUS STUFFS SUITED FOR SEA TRADE AND REPAIRED WITH THEM FROM BAGHDAD CITY TO BASSORAH TOWN WHERE I FOUND A SHIP READY FOR SEA AND IN HER A COMPANY OF CONSIDERABLE MERCHANTS
hypothesis: So having made up my mind, I packed up in bales a quantity of precious stuffs suited for sea trade and repaired with them from Baghdad city to Basra town where I found a ship ready for sea.

reference: BUT THE CAPTAIN AROSE AND TIGHTENING HIS GIRDLE TUCKED UP HIS SKIRTS AND AFTER TAKING REFUGE WITH ALLAH FROM SATAN THE STONED CLOMB TO THE MAST HEAD WHENCE HE LOOKED OUT RIGHT AND LEFT AND GAZING AT THE PASSENGERS AND CREW FELL TO BUFFETING HIS FACE AND PLUCKING OUT HIS BEARD
hypothesis: But the captain arose and tightening his girdle, tucked up his skirts and after taking refuge with Allah from Satan the Stoned, climbed to the masthead whence he looked out right and left and gazing at the passengers and crew, fell to buffeting his face and plucking out his beard.

reference: THIS HE SET IN A SAUCER WETTED WITH A LITTLE WATER AND AFTER WAITING A SHORT TIME SMELT AND TASTED IT AND THEN HE TOOK OUT OF THE CHEST A BOOKLET WHEREIN HE READ AWHILE AND SAID WEEPING KNOW O YE PASSENGERS THAT IN THIS BOOK IS A MARVELLOUS MATTER DENOTING THAT WHOSO COMETH HITHER SHALL SURELY DIE WITHOUT HOPE OF ESCAPE FOR THAT THIS OCEAN IS CALLED THE SEA OF THE CLIME OF THE KING WHEREIN IS THE SEPULCHRE OF OUR LORD SOLOMON SON OF DAVID ON BOTH BE PEACE
hypothesis: This he set in a saucer wetted with a little water, and after waiting a short time smelt and tasted it.

reference: ALTHOUGH THE PLAGUE WAS THERE IN THE MOST PART OF ALL THE HOUSES THEY NEVERTHELESS ENTERED EVERYWHERE THEN PLUNDERED AND CARRIED AWAY ALL THAT WAS WITHIN AND YET FOR ALL THIS NOT ONE OF THEM TOOK ANY HURT WHICH IS A MOST WONDERFUL CASE
hypothesis: Although the plague was there in the most part of all the houses, they nevertheless entered everywhere, then plundered and carried away all that was within.

hypothesis: My poor Lady Vavasor is carried to the Tower and her great belly could not excuse her because she was acquainted by somebody that there was a plot against the Protector.

hypothesis: I never saw anyone yet that did not look simply and out of countenance, nor ever knew a wedding well designed but one, and that was of two persons who had time enough, I confess, to contrive it, and nobody to please in it but themselves.

hypothesis: The truth is, I could not endure to be Mrs. Bride in a public wedding, to be made the happiest person on earth.

hypothesis: Yet in earnest, your father will not find my brother Peyton wanting in civility, though he is not a man of much compliment, unless it be in his letters to me.

reference: YOU SEE I GIVE YOU FAIR WARNING

reference: HERE ARE SOME VERSES OF COWLEY'S TELL ME HOW YOU LIKE THEM

hypothesis: I told you in my last that my Suffolk journey was laid aside and that into Kent hastened.

hypothesis: If I drown, by the way, this will be my last letter and like a will, I bequeath all my kindness to you in it.

hypothesis: Indeed, I like him extremely and he is commended to me by people that know him very well and are able to judge for a most excellent servant and faithful as possible.

hypothesis: Because you find fault with my other letters. This is like to be shorter than they. I did not intend it so though, I can assure you.

reference: I DO NOT FIND IT THOUGH I AM TOLD I WAS SO EXTREMELY WHEN I BELIEVED YOU LOVED ME
hypothesis: I do not find it though. I am told I was so extremely when I believed you love me.

hypothesis: But I am called upon

hypothesis: Directed for your master.

hypothesis: I see you can chid when you please.

reference: WE DARE NOT LET OUR TONGUES LIE MORE ON ONE SIDE OF OUR MOUTHS THAN T'OTHER FOR FEAR OF OVERTURNING IT

reference: YOU ARE SATISFIED I HOPE ERE THIS THAT I SCAPED DROWNING

reference: WILL YOU BE SO GOOD NATURED
hypothesis: Will you be so good natured?

hypothesis: He has one son and he's the finest boy that e'er you saw and has a noble spirit, but yet stands in that awe of his father that one word from him is as much as 20 whippings.

hypothesis: You must give me leave to entertain you thus with discourses of the family, for I can tell you nothing else from hence.

hypothesis: In my life I never heard so ridiculous a discourse as he made us, and no old woman who passes for a witch could have been more puzzled to see what to say to reasonable people than he was.

hypothesis: Mr. Fish is a squire of dames and has so many mistresses that anybody may pretend a share in him and be believed. But though I have the honour to be his near neighbor, to speak freely, I cannot brag much that he makes any court to me, and I know no young woman in the country that he does not visit often.

hypothesis: I think my youngest brother comes down with him.

hypothesis: But it seems this gentleman is not so easy access.

hypothesis: I am here much more out of people's way than in town where my aunt and such has pretend an interest in me and a power over me. Do so persecute me with dear good nature.

hypothesis: Well,

hypothesis: The matter is not great for I confess I do naturally hate the noise and talk of the world and should be best pleased never to be known in upon any occasion whatsoever yet.

hypothesis: I have been studying how Tom Cheek might come by his intelligence and I verily believe he has it from my cousin Peters.

hypothesis: How kindly do I take the civilities of your fathers in earnest. You cannot imagine how his letter pleased me.

hypothesis: As they were in no haste and the confinement of a railroad car would be very irksome to the younger children, it had been decided to make the journey by water.

hypothesis: There were no sad leave takings to mar their pleasure. The children were in wild spirits, and all seemed cheerful and happy as they sat or stood upon the deck watching the receding shore as the vessel steamed out of the harbor.

hypothesis: At length the land had quite disappeared. Nothing could be seen but the sky overhead and a vast expanse of water all round, and the passengers found leisure to turn their attention upon each other.

reference: DOUBTLESS THAT IS THE CASE REMARKED MISTER DINSMORE

hypothesis: Is it strange then that smokers and chewers have a thousand ailments?

hypothesis: That the French Polytechnic Institute had to prohibit its use on account of its effects upon the mind.

reference: NOTICE THE MULTITUDE OF SUDDEN DEATHS AND SEE HOW MANY ARE SMOKERS AND CHEWERS
hypothesis: Notice the multitude of sudden deaths and see how many are smokers and chewers.

hypothesis: In a small country town, seven of these mysterious providences occurred within the circuit of a mile, all directly traceable to tobacco. And any physician on a few moments reflection can match this fact by his own observation.

reference: AND THEN SUCH POWERFUL ACIDS PRODUCE INTENSE IRRITATION AND THIRST THIRST WHICH WATER DOES NOT QUENCH
hypothesis: And then such powerful acids produce intense irritation and thirst, thirst which water does not quench.

reference: HENCE A RESORT TO CIDER AND BEER
hypothesis: Hence a resort to cider and beer.

hypothesis: No sir. What know ye not that y your body is the temple of the Holy Ghost which is in you, which ye have of God, and ye are not your own.

hypothesis: For you are bought with a price. Therefore glorify God in your body and in your spirit, which are God's.

hypothesis: We certainly have no right to injure our bodies either by neglect or self indulgence.

hypothesis: And again, I beseech you therefore, brethren, by the mercies of God, that ye present your bodies a living sacrifice, holy, acceptable unto God, which is your reasonable service.

reference: MY FATHER WAS IN THE EMPLOY OF THE UNITED STATES GOVERNMENT AND HAD THE MAIL CONTRACT FOR FIVE HUNDRED MILES
hypothesis: My father was in the employ of the United States government and had the mail contract for 500 mi.

reference: HE HAD STARTED BACK TO HARRISONVILLE IN A BUGGY BUT WAS WAYLAID ONE MILE SOUTH OF WESTPORT A SUBURB OF KANSAS CITY AND BRUTALLY MURDERED FALLING OUT OF HIS BUGGY INTO THE ROAD WITH THREE MORTAL BULLET WOUNDS
hypothesis: He had started back to Harrisville in a buggy but was waylaid 1 mi south of Westport, a suburb of Kansas City, and brutally murdered, falling out of his buggy into the road with three mortal bullet wounds.

reference: MISSUS WASHINGTON WELLS AND HER SON SAMUEL ON THE ROAD HOME FROM KANSAS CITY TO LEE'S SUMMIT RECOGNIZED THE BODY AS THAT OF MY FATHER
hypothesis: Miss Washington Wells and her son Samuel on the Ro home from Kansas City to Lee's Summit recognized the body as that of my father.

reference: MISSUS WELLS STAYED TO GUARD THE REMAINS WHILE HER SON CARRIED THE NEWS OF THE MURDER TO COLONEL PEABODY OF THE FEDERAL COMMAND WHO WAS THEN IN CAMP AT KANSAS CITY
hypothesis: Miss Wells stayed to guard the remains while her son carried the news of the murder to Colonel Peabody of the Federal command, who was then in camp at Kansas City.

reference: MISSUS MC CORKLE JUMPED FROM THE WINDOW OF THE HOUSE AND ESCAPED
hypothesis: Miss Mc Cork jumped from the window of the house and escaped.

reference: AS THE RAIDERS LEFT ONE OF THEM SHOUTED
hypothesis: As the raiders left, one of them shouted.

reference: NOW OLD LADY CALL ON YOUR PROTECTORS WHY DON'T YOU CALL ON COLE YOUNGER NOW
hypothesis: Now old lady, call on your protectors. Why don't you call on Cole Younger now?

reference: EVERY KNOT REPRESENTED A HUMAN LIFE
hypothesis: Every night represented a human life.

reference: BUT SHE FAILED TO FIND THE COMFORT SHE SOUGHT FOR ANNOYANCES CONTINUED IN A MORE AGGRAVATED FORM
hypothesis: But she failed to find the comfort she sought for. Annoyances continued in a more aggravated form.

reference: TWO MONTHS AFTER THIS INCIDENT THE SAME PERSECUTORS AGAIN ENTERED OUR HOME IN THE DEAD OF THE NIGHT AND AT THE POINT OF A PISTOL TRIED TO FORCE MY MOTHER TO SET FIRE TO HER OWN HOME
hypothesis: Two months after this incident, the same persecutors again entered our home in the dead of the night and at the point of a pistol tried to force my mother to set fire to her own home.

reference: I HAVE ALWAYS FELT THAT THE EXPOSURE TO WHICH SHE WAS SUBJECTED ON THIS CRUEL JOURNEY TOO HARD EVEN FOR A MAN TO TAKE WAS THE DIRECT CAUSE OF HER DEATH
hypothesis: I have always felt that the exposure to which she was subjected on this cruel journey, too hard even for a man to take, was the direct cause of her death.

reference: FROM HARRISONVILLE SHE WENT TO WAVERLY WHERE SHE WAS HOUNDED CONTINUALLY
hypothesis: From Harrisville, she went to Waverly where she was hounded continually.

reference: ONE OF THE CONDITIONS UPON WHICH HER LIFE WAS SPARED WAS THAT SHE WOULD REPORT AT LEXINGTON WEEKLY
hypothesis: One of the conditions upon which her life was spared was that she would report at Lexington weekly.

reference: ONE OF MY OLD SCHOOL TEACHERS WHOM I HAVE NEVER SEEN SINCE THE SPRING OR SUMMER OF EIGHTEEN SIXTY TWO IS STEPHEN B ELKINS SENATOR FROM WEST VIRGINIA
hypothesis: One of my old school teachers, whom I have never seen since the spring or summer of 1862, is Stephen B. Elkins, Senator from West Virginia.

reference: WHEN I WAS TAKEN PRISONER I EXPECTED TO BE SHOT WITHOUT CEREMONY
hypothesis: When I was taken prisoner, I expected to be shot without ceremony.

reference: ALTHOUGH EVERY BOOK PURPORTING TO NARRATE THE LIVES OF THE YOUNGER BROTHERS HAS TOLD OF THE LIBERTY ROBBERY AND IMPLIED THAT WE HAD A PART IN IT THE YOUNGERS WERE NOT SUSPECTED AT THAT TIME NOR FOR A LONG TIME AFTERWARD
hypothesis: Although every book purporting to narrate the lives of the Younger brothers as told of the Liberty robbery and implied that we had a part in it, the Youngs were not suspected at that time nor for a long time afterward.

reference: IT WAS CLAIMED BY PEOPLE OF LIBERTY THAT THEY POSITIVELY RECOGNIZED AMONG THE ROBBERS OLL SHEPHERD RED MONKERS AND BUD PENCE WHO HAD SEEN SERVICE WITH QUANTRELL
hypothesis: It was claimed by people of liberty that they positively recognized among the robbers all Shepherd Red Mockers and Bud Pence, who had seen service with Quantrell.

reference: THIS RAID WAS ACCOMPANIED BY BLOODSHED JUDGE MC LAIN THE BANKER BEING SHOT THOUGH NOT FATALLY
hypothesis: This raid was accompanied by bloodshed, Judge Mclean, the banker, being shot, though not fatally.

reference: NO WARRANT WAS ISSUED FOR THE YOUNGERS BUT SUBSEQUENT HISTORIANS HAVE INFERENTIALLY AT LEAST ACCUSED US OF TAKING PART BUT AS I SAID BEFORE THERE IS NO TRUTH IN THE ACCUSATION
hypothesis: No warrant was issued for the younger, but subsequent historians have, inferential at least, accused us of taking part. But as I said before, there is no truth in the accusation.

reference: JUNE THIRD EIGHTEEN SEVENTY ONE OBOCOCK BROTHERS BANK AT CORYDON IOWA WAS ROBBED OF FORTY THOUSAND DOLLARS BY SEVEN MEN IN BROAD DAYLIGHT
hypothesis: June 3, 1871, Ocock Brothers Bank at Croydon, Iowa, was robbed of $40000 by seven men in broad daylight.

reference: IT WAS CHARGED THAT ARTHUR MC COY OR A C MC COY AND MYSELF HAD BEEN PARTICIPANTS IN THE GAD'S HILL AFFAIR AND THE TWO STAGE ROBBERIES
hypothesis: It was charged that Arthur Mccoy or A. C. Mccoy and myself had been participants in the Gas Hill affair and the two stage robberies.

reference: THE PARTS OF THIS LETTER NOW RELEVANT ARE AS FOLLOWS
hypothesis: The parts of this letter now relevant are as follows.

reference: YOU MAY USE THIS LETTER IN YOUR OWN WAY
hypothesis: You may use this letter in your own way.

reference: I WILL GIVE YOU THIS OUTLINE AND SKETCH OF MY WHEREABOUTS AND ACTIONS AT THE TIME OF CERTAIN ROBBERIES WITH WHICH I AM CHARGED
hypothesis: I will give you this outline and sketch of my whereabouts and actions at the time of certain robberies with which I am charged.

reference: AT THE TIME OF THE GALLATIN BANK ROBBERY I WAS GATHERING CATTLE IN ELLIS COUNTY TEXAS CATTLE THAT I BOUGHT FROM PLEAS TAYLOR AND RECTOR
hypothesis: At the time of the Gallatin bank robbery, I was gathering cattle in Ellis County, Texas, cattle that I bought from Perez, Taylor, and Rector.

reference: THIS CAN BE PROVED BY BOTH OF THEM ALSO BY SHERIFF BARKLEY AND FIFTY OTHER RESPECTABLE MEN OF THAT COUNTY
hypothesis: This can be proved by both of them, also by Sheriff Barkley and 50 other respectable men of that county.

reference: I BROUGHT THE CATTLE TO KANSAS THAT FALL AND REMAINED IN SAINT CLAIR COUNTY UNTIL FEBRUARY
hypothesis: I brought the cattle to Kansas that fall and remained in St. Clair County until February.

reference: I THEN WENT TO ARKANSAS AND RETURNED TO SAINT CLAIR COUNTY ABOUT THE FIRST OF MAY
hypothesis: I then went to Arkansas and returned to St. Clair County about 1 May.

reference: I WENT TO KANSAS WHERE OUR CATTLE WERE IN WOODSON COUNTY AT COLONEL RIDGE'S
hypothesis: I went to Kansas where our cattle were in Woodson County at Colonel Ridges.

reference: DURING THE SUMMER I WAS EITHER IN SAINT CLAIR JACKSON OR KANSAS BUT AS THERE WAS NO ROBBERY COMMITTED THAT SUMMER IT MAKES NO DIFFERENCE WHERE I WAS
hypothesis: During the summer I was either in St., Clair, Jackson, or Kansas, but as there was no robbery committed that summer, it makes no difference where I was.

reference: I WENT THROUGH INDEPENDENCE AND FROM THERE TO ACE WEBB'S
hypothesis: I went through Independence and from there to Ace Webs.

reference: THERE I TOOK DINNER AND THEN WENT TO DOCTOR L W TWYMAN'S
hypothesis: There I took dinner and then went to Dr.. L. W.

reference: OUR BUSINESS THERE WAS TO SEE E P WEST HE WAS NOT AT HOME BUT THE FAMILY WILL REMEMBER THAT WE WERE THERE
hypothesis: Our business there was to see E. P. West. He was not at home, but the family will remember that we were there.

reference: WE CROSSED ON THE BRIDGE STAYED IN THE CITY ALL NIGHT AND THE NEXT MORNING WE RODE UP THROUGH THE CITY
hypothesis: We crossed on the bridge, stayed in the city all night, and the next morning we rode up through the city.

reference: I MET SEVERAL OF MY FRIENDS AMONG THEM WAS BOB HUDSPETH
hypothesis: I met several of my friends, among them was Bob Huth.

reference: WE WERE NOT ON GOOD TERMS AT THE TIME NOR HAVE WE BEEN FOR SEVERAL YEARS
hypothesis: We were not on good terms at the time, nor have we been for several years.

reference: POOR JOHN HE HAS BEEN HUNTED DOWN AND SHOT LIKE A WILD BEAST AND NEVER WAS A BOY MORE INNOCENT
hypothesis: Poor John, he has been hunted down and shot like a wild beast, and never was a boy more innocent.

reference: DOCTOR L LEWIS WAS HIS PHYSICIAN
hypothesis: Dr.. L. Lewis was his physician.

reference: THERE WERE FIFTY OR A HUNDRED PERSONS THERE WHO WILL TESTIFY IN ANY COURT THAT JOHN AND I WERE THERE
hypothesis: There were 50 or a hundred persons there who will testify in any court that John and I were there.

reference: HELVIN FICKLE AND WIFE OF GREENTON VALLEY WERE ATTENDING THE SPRINGS AT THAT TIME AND EITHER OF THEM WILL TESTIFY TO THE ABOVE FOR JOHN AND I SAT IN FRONT OF MISTER SMITH WHILE HE WAS PREACHING AND WAS IN HIS COMPANY FOR A FEW MOMENTS TOGETHER WITH HIS WIFE AND MISTER AND MISSUS FICKLE AFTER SERVICE
hypothesis: Elvin Fickle and wife of Greens and Valley were attending the springs at that time and either of them will testify to the above for John and I sat in front of Mr. Smith while he was preaching and was in his company for a few moments together with his wife and Mr. and Miss Fickle after the service.

reference: ABOUT THE LAST OF DECEMBER EIGHTEEN SEVENTY THREE I ARRIVED IN CARROLL PARISH LOUISIANA
hypothesis: About the last of December, 1873, I arrived in Carroll Parish, Louisiana.

reference: I STAYED THERE UNTIL THE EIGHTH OF FEBRUARY EIGHTEEN SEVENTY FOUR
hypothesis: I stayed there until 8 February 1874.

reference: I HAD NOT HEARD OF THAT WHEN I WROTE THE LETTER OF EIGHTEEN SEVENTY FOUR AND TO CORRECT ANY MISAPPREHENSION THAT MIGHT BE CREATED BY OMITTING IT I WILL SAY THAT AT THAT TIME I WAS AT NEOSHO KANSAS WITH A DROVE OF CATTLE WHICH I SOLD TO MAJOR RAY
hypothesis: I had not heard of that when I wrote the letter of 1874. And to correct any misapprehension that might be created by omitting it, I will say that at the time I was at Neosho, Kansas, with a drove of cattle which I sold to Major Ray.

reference: IT WAS IMMEDIATELY FOLLOWING THE ROCK ISLAND ROBBERY AT ADAIR IOWA THAT THERE FIRST APPEARED A DELIBERATE ENLISTMENT OF SOME LOCAL PAPERS IN MISSOURI TO CONNECT US WITH THIS ROBBERY
hypothesis: It was immediately following the Rock Island robbery at Adair, Iowa, that there 1st appeared a deliberate enlistment of some local papers in Missouri to connect us with this robbery.

reference: WE TOOK THE OATH PERHAPS THREE HUNDRED OF US DOWN ON LUTHER MASON'S FARM A FEW MILES FROM WHERE I NOW WRITE WHERE COLONEL HAYS HAD ENCAMPED AFTER INDEPENDENCE
hypothesis: We took the oath, perhaps 300 of us, down on Luther Mason's farm, a few miles from where I now write, where Colonel Hayes had encamped after independence.

reference: BOONE MUIR AND MYSELF MET COFFEE AND THE REST BELOW ROSE HILL ON GRAND RIVER
hypothesis: Boone, Ewer and myself met Coffee and the rest below Rose Hill on Grand River.

reference: ACCORDINGLY I WAS SHORTLY AWAKENED TO ACCOMPANY HIM TO LONE JACK WHERE HE WOULD PERSONALLY MAKE KNOWN THE SITUATION TO THE OTHER COLONELS
hypothesis: Accordingly, I was shortly awakened to accompany him to Loan Jack, where he would personally make known the situation to the other colonels.

reference: FOSTER HAD NEARLY ONE THOUSAND CAVALRYMEN AND TWO PIECES OF RABB'S INDIANA BATTERY THAT HAD ALREADY MADE FOR ITSELF A NAME FOR HARD FIGHTING
hypothesis: Foster had nearly 1000 cavalrymen and two pieces of raves. Indiana Battery that had already made for itself a name for hard fighting.

reference: COME IN COLONEL HAYS EXCLAIMED COLONEL COCKRELL
hypothesis: Come in, Colonel Hayes, exclaimed Colonel Cockrell.

reference: I THINK HE'LL BE RATHER TOUGH MEAT FOR BREAKFAST I REPLIED HE MIGHT BE ALL RIGHT FOR DINNER
hypothesis: I think he'll be rather tough meat for breakfast, I replied. He might be all right for dinner.

reference: JACKMAN WITH A PARTY OF THIRTY SEASONED MEN CHARGED THE INDIANA GUNS AND CAPTURED THEM BUT MAJOR FOSTER LED A GALLANT CHARGE AGAINST THE INVADERS AND RECAPTURED THE PIECES
hypothesis: Jackman, with a party of 30 seasoned men charged the Indiana guns and captured them, but Major Foster led a gallant charge against the invaders and recaptured the pieces.

reference: WE WERE OUT OF AMMUNITION AND WERE HELPLESS HAD THE FIGHT BEEN PRESSED
hypothesis: We were out of ammunition and were helpless. Had the fight been pressed.

reference: THEY DID MARK MY CLOTHES IN ONE OR TWO PLACES HOWEVER
hypothesis: They did mark my clothes in one or two places, however.

reference: MAJOR FOSTER IN A LETTER TO JUDGE GEORGE M BENNETT OF MINNEAPOLIS SAID
hypothesis: Major Foster, in a letter to Judge George M. Bennett of Minneapolis, said

reference: I WAS TOLD BY SOME OF OUR MEN FROM THE WESTERN BORDER OF THE STATE THAT THEY RECOGNIZED THE DARING YOUNG RIDER AS COLE YOUNGER
hypothesis: I was told by some of our men from the western border of the state that they recognize the daring young writer as Cole Younger.

reference: ABOUT NINE THIRTY A M I WAS SHOT DOWN
hypothesis: About 09:30 a.m., I was shot down.

reference: THE WOUNDED OF BOTH FORCES WERE GATHERED UP AND WERE PLACED IN HOUSES
hypothesis: The wounded of both forces were gathered up and were placed in houses.

reference: THAT NIGHT IT STARTED TO RAIN AND WE WORE OUT OUR HORSES
hypothesis: That night it started to rain and we wore out our horses.

reference: FRIDAY WE MOVED TOWARD WATERVILLE AND FRIDAY NIGHT WE CAMPED BETWEEN ELYSIAN AND GERMAN LAKE
hypothesis: Friday we moved toward Waterville and Friday night we camped between Elysian and German Lake.

reference: BOB'S SHATTERED ELBOW WAS REQUIRING FREQUENT ATTENTION AND THAT NIGHT WE MADE ONLY NINE MILES AND MONDAY MONDAY NIGHT AND TUESDAY WE SPENT IN A DESERTED FARM HOUSE CLOSE TO MANKATO
hypothesis: Bob's shattered elbow was requiring frequent attention and that night we made only 9 mi. And Monday, Monday night and Tuesday we spent in a deserted farmhouse close to Mankato.

reference: THAT DAY A MAN NAMED DUNNING DISCOVERED US AND WE TOOK HIM PRISONER
hypothesis: That day a man named Dunning discovered us and we took him prisoner.

reference: FINALLY WE ADMINISTERED TO HIM AN OATH NOT TO BETRAY OUR WHEREABOUTS UNTIL WE HAD TIME TO MAKE OUR ESCAPE AND HE AGREED NOT TO
hypothesis: Finally, we administered to him an oath not to betray our whereabouts until we had time to make our escape, and he agreed not to.

reference: NO SOONER HOWEVER WAS HE RELEASED THAN HE MADE POSTHASTE INTO MANKATO TO ANNOUNCE OUR PRESENCE AND IN A FEW MINUTES ANOTHER POSSE WAS LOOKING FOR US
hypothesis: No sooner, however, was he released than he made post haste to Mankato to announce our presence, and in a few minutes another posse was looking for us.

reference: THE WHISTLE ON THE OIL MILL BLEW AND WE FEARED THAT IT WAS A SIGNAL THAT HAD BEEN AGREED UPON TO ALARM THE TOWN IN CASE WE WERE OBSERVED BUT WE WERE NOT MOLESTED
hypothesis: The whistle on the oil mill blew and we feared that it was a signal that had been agreed upon to alarm the town in case we were observed. but we were not molested.

reference: HE HAD TO SLEEP WITH IT PILLOWED ON MY BREAST JIM BEING ALSO CRIPPLED WITH A WOUND IN HIS SHOULDER AND WE COULD NOT GET MUCH SLEEP
hypothesis: He had to sleep with that pillow on my breast, Jim being also a cripple with a wound in his shoulder and we could not get much sleep.

reference: BUT THEY SOON AFTER GOT CLOSE ENOUGH SO THAT ONE OF THEM BROKE MY WALKING STICK WITH A SHOT
hypothesis: But they soon after got close enough so that one of them broke my walking stick with a shot.

reference: WE WERE IN SIGHT OF OUR LONG SOUGHT HORSES WHEN THEY CUT US OFF FROM THE ANIMALS AND OUR LAST HOPE WAS GONE
hypothesis: We were in sight of our long sawed horses when they cut us off from the animals and our last hope was gone.

reference: SIX STEPPED TO THE FRONT SHERIFF GLISPIN COLONEL T L VOUGHT B M RICE G A BRADFORD C A POMEROY AND S J SEVERSON
hypothesis: Six steps to the front, Sheriff, Gsp, Colonel T. L. Vaught, B. M. Rice, G. A. Bradford, C. A. Pomeroy and S. J. Severs.

reference: FORMING IN LINE FOUR PACES APART HE ORDERED THEM TO ADVANCE RAPIDLY AND CONCENTRATE THE FIRE OF THE WHOLE LINE THE INSTANT THE ROBBERS WERE DISCOVERED
hypothesis: Forming in line four paces apart, he ordered them to advance rapidly and concentrate the fire of the whole line the instant the robbers were discovered.

reference: MAKE FOR THE HORSES I SAID EVERY MAN FOR HIMSELF
hypothesis: Make for the horses. I said every man for himself.

reference: THERE IS NO USE STOPPING TO PICK UP A COMRADE HERE FOR WE CAN'T GET HIM THROUGH THE LINE JUST CHARGE THEM AND MAKE IT IF WE CAN
hypothesis: There's no use stopping to pick up a comrade here for we can't get him through the line. Just charge them and make it if we can.

reference: I GOT UP AS THE SIGNAL FOR THE CHARGE AND WE FIRED ONE VOLLEY
hypothesis: I got up as a signal for the charge and we fired one volley.

reference: ONE OF THE FELLOWS IN THE OUTER LINE NOT BRAVE ENOUGH HIMSELF TO JOIN THE VOLUNTEERS WHO HAD COME IN TO BEAT US OUT WAS NOT DISPOSED TO BELIEVE IN THE SURRENDER AND HAD HIS GUN LEVELLED ON BOB IN SPITE OF THE HANDKERCHIEF WHICH WAS WAVING AS A FLAG OF TRUCE
hypothesis: One of the fellows in the outer line, not brave enough himself to join the volunteers who had come in to beat us out, was not disposed to believe in the surrender and had his gun leveled on Bob in spite of the handkerchief which was waving as a flag of truce.

reference: SHERIFF GLISPIN OF WATONWAN COUNTY WHO WAS TAKING BOB'S PISTOL FROM HIM WAS ALSO SHOUTING TO THE FELLOW
hypothesis: Sheriff Gsp of Water One County who was taking Bob's pistol from him was also shouting to the fellow.

reference: INCLUDING THOSE RECEIVED IN AND ON THE WAY FROM NORTHFIELD I HAD ELEVEN WOUNDS
hypothesis: Including those received in and on the way from Northfield, I had eleven wounds.

reference: AND SHERIFF GLISPIN'S ORDER NOT TO SHOOT WAS THE BEGINNING OF THE PROTECTORATE THAT MINNESOTA PEOPLE ESTABLISHED OVER US
hypothesis: And Sheriff Grisons order not to shoot was the beginning of the protectorate that Minnesota people established over us.

reference: I URGED ON THE BOYS THAT WHATEVER HAPPENED WE SHOULD NOT SHOOT ANY ONE
hypothesis: I urged on the boys that whatever happened, we should not shoot anyone.

reference: WHEN MILLER AND I CROSSED THE BRIDGE THE THREE WERE ON SOME DRY GOODS BOXES AT THE CORNER NEAR THE BANK AND AS SOON AS THEY SAW US WENT RIGHT INTO THE BANK INSTEAD OF WAITING FOR US TO GET THERE
hypothesis: When Miller and I crossed the bridge, the three were on some dry good boxes at the corner near the bank and as soon as they saw us went right into the bank instead of waiting for us to get there.

reference: WHEN WE CAME UP I TOLD MILLER TO SHUT THE BANK DOOR WHICH THEY HAD LEFT OPEN IN THEIR HURRY
hypothesis: When we came up, I told Miller to shut the bank door, which they had left open in their hurry.

reference: J S ALLEN WHOSE HARDWARE STORE WAS NEAR TRIED TO GO INTO THE BANK BUT MILLER ORDERED HIM AWAY AND HE RAN AROUND THE CORNER SHOUTING
hypothesis: J. S. Allen, whose hardware store was near, tried to go into the bank, but Miller ordered him away and he ran around the corner shouting.

reference: GET YOUR GUNS BOYS THEY'RE ROBBING THE BANK
hypothesis: Get your guns boys, they're robbing the bank.

reference: AND I CALLED TO HIM TO GET INSIDE AT THE SAME TIME FIRING A PISTOL SHOT IN THE AIR AS A SIGNAL TO THE THREE BOYS AT THE BRIDGE THAT WE HAD BEEN DISCOVERED
hypothesis: And I called to him to get inside, at the same time firing a pistol shot in the air as a signal to the three boys at the bridge that we had been discovered.

reference: ALMOST AT THIS INSTANT I HEARD A PISTOL SHOT IN THE BANK
hypothesis: Almost at this instant I heard a pistol shot in the bank.

reference: CHADWELL WOODS AND JIM RODE UP AND JOINED US SHOUTING TO PEOPLE IN THE STREET TO GET INSIDE AND FIRING THEIR PISTOLS TO EMPHASIZE THEIR COMMANDS
hypothesis: Chadwell, Woods and Jim rode up and joined us, shouting to the people in the street to get inside and firing their pistols to emphasize their commands.

reference: IF ANY OF OUR PARTY SHOT HIM IT MUST HAVE BEEN WOODS
hypothesis: If any of our party shot him, it must have been Woods.

reference: MEANTIME THE STREET WAS GETTING UNCOMFORTABLY HOT
hypothesis: Meantime, the street was getting uncomfortably hot.

reference: EVERY TIME I SAW ANY ONE WITH A BEAD ON ME I WOULD DROP OFF MY HORSE AND TRY TO DRIVE THE SHOOTER INSIDE BUT I COULD NOT SEE IN EVERY DIRECTION
hypothesis: Every time I saw anyone with a bead on me, I would drop off my horse and try to drive the shooter inside, but I could not see in every direction.

reference: DOCTOR WHEELER WHO HAD GONE UPSTAIRS IN THE HOTEL SHOT MILLER AND HE LAY DYING IN THE STREET
hypothesis: Dr., Wheeler, who had gone upstairs in the hotel, shot Miller and he lay dying in the street.

reference: CHANGING HIS PISTOL TO HIS LEFT HAND BOB RAN OUT AND MOUNTED MILLER'S MARE
hypothesis: Changing his pistol to his left hand, Bob ran out and mounted Miller's mare.

reference: WHAT KEPT YOU SO LONG I ASKED PITTS
hypothesis: What kept you so long? I asked Pitts.

reference: AS TO THE REST OF THE AFFAIR INSIDE THE BANK I TAKE THE ACCOUNT OF A NORTHFIELD NARRATOR
hypothesis: As to the rest of the affair inside the bank, I take the account of a Northfield narrator.

reference: WHERE'S THE MONEY OUTSIDE THE SAFE BOB ASKED
hypothesis: Where's the money outside the safe? Bob asked.

reference: THE SHUTTERS WERE CLOSED AND THIS CAUSED BUNKER AN INSTANT'S DELAY THAT WAS ALMOST FATAL PITTS CHASED HIM WITH A BULLET
hypothesis: The shutters were closed and this caused Bunker an instance delay that was almost fatal. Fitz chased him with a bullet.

reference: THE FIRST ONE MISSED HIM BUT THE SECOND WENT THROUGH HIS RIGHT SHOULDER
hypothesis: The 1st one missed him, but the 2nd went through his right shoulder.

reference: THEY SWARMED UP IN FRONT OF SHERBURN'S PALINGS AS THICK AS THEY COULD JAM TOGETHER AND YOU COULDN'T HEAR YOURSELF THINK FOR THE NOISE
hypothesis: They swarmed up in front of Sherburne palings as thick as they could jam together and you couldn't hear yourself think for the noise.

reference: SOME SUNG OUT TEAR DOWN THE FENCE TEAR DOWN THE FENCE
hypothesis: Some sung out, tear down the fence, tear down the fence.

reference: THE STILLNESS WAS AWFUL CREEPY AND UNCOMFORTABLE
hypothesis: The stillness was awful creepy and uncomfortable.

reference: SHERBURN RUN HIS EYE SLOW ALONG THE CROWD AND WHEREVER IT STRUCK THE PEOPLE TRIED A LITTLE TO OUT GAZE HIM BUT THEY COULDN'T THEY DROPPED THEIR EYES AND LOOKED SNEAKY
hypothesis: Sherburn run his eye slow along the crowd and wherever it struck the people tried a little to out gaze him but they couldn't. They dropped their eyes and looked sneaky.

reference: THE AVERAGE MAN'S A COWARD
hypothesis: The average man's a coward.

reference: BECAUSE THEY'RE AFRAID THE MAN'S FRIENDS WILL SHOOT THEM IN THE BACK IN THE DARKAND IT'S JUST WHAT THEY WOULD DO
hypothesis: Because they're afraid the man's friends will shoot them in the back in the dark and it's just what they would do.

reference: SO THEY ALWAYS ACQUIT AND THEN A MAN GOES IN THE NIGHT WITH A HUNDRED MASKED COWARDS AT HIS BACK AND LYNCHES THE RASCAL
hypothesis: So they always acquit. And then a man goes in the night with a hundred masked cowards at his back and Lynch is the rascal.

reference: YOU DIDN'T WANT TO COME
hypothesis: You didn't want to come.

reference: BUT A MOB WITHOUT ANY MAN AT THE HEAD OF IT IS BENEATH PITIFULNESS
hypothesis: But a mob without any man at the head of it is beneath pitiful.

reference: NOW LEAVE AND TAKE YOUR HALF A MAN WITH YOU TOSSING HIS GUN UP ACROSS HIS LEFT ARM AND COCKING IT WHEN HE SAYS THIS
hypothesis: Now leave and take your half a man with you.

reference: THE CROWD WASHED BACK SUDDEN AND THEN BROKE ALL APART AND WENT TEARING OFF EVERY WHICH WAY AND BUCK HARKNESS HE HEELED IT AFTER THEM LOOKING TOLERABLE CHEAP
hypothesis: The crowd washed back sudden and then broke all apart and went tearing off every which way. And Buck Harkness, he heeled it after them looking tolerable cheap.

reference: YOU CAN'T BE TOO CAREFUL
hypothesis: You can't be too careful.

reference: THEY ARGUED AND TRIED TO KEEP HIM OUT BUT HE WOULDN'T LISTEN AND THE WHOLE SHOW COME TO A STANDSTILL
hypothesis: They argued and tried to keep him out, but he wouldn't listen and the whole show come to a standstill.

reference: AND ONE OR TWO WOMEN BEGUN TO SCREAM
hypothesis: And one or two women begin to scream.

reference: SO THEN THE RINGMASTER HE MADE A LITTLE SPEECH AND SAID HE HOPED THERE WOULDN'T BE NO DISTURBANCE AND IF THE MAN WOULD PROMISE HE WOULDN'T MAKE NO MORE TROUBLE HE WOULD LET HIM RIDE IF HE THOUGHT HE COULD STAY ON THE HORSE
hypothesis: So then the ringmaster, he made a little speech and said he hoped there wouldn't be no disturbance and if the man would promise he wouldn't make no more trouble, he would let him ride if he thought he could stay on the horse.

reference: IT WARN'T FUNNY TO ME THOUGH I WAS ALL OF A TREMBLE TO SEE HIS DANGER
hypothesis: It warn't funny to me though. I was all of a tremble to see his danger.

reference: AND THE HORSE A GOING LIKE A HOUSE AFIRE TOO
hypothesis: And the horse of going like a house afire too.

reference: HE SHED THEM SO THICK THEY KIND OF CLOGGED UP THE AIR AND ALTOGETHER HE SHED SEVENTEEN SUITS
hypothesis: He shed them so thick they kind of clogged up the air and altogether he shed 17 suits.

reference: WHY IT WAS ONE OF HIS OWN MEN
hypothesis: Why, it was one of his own men.

reference: WHICH WAS SOUND ENOUGH JUDGMENT BUT YOU TAKE THE AVERAGE MAN AND HE WOULDN'T WAIT FOR HIM TO HOWL
hypothesis: Which was sound enough judgment, but you take the average man and he wouldn't wait for him to how.

reference: THE KING'S DUDS WAS ALL BLACK AND HE DID LOOK REAL SWELL AND STARCHY
hypothesis: The king's duds was all black and he did look real swell and starchy.

reference: WHY BEFORE HE LOOKED LIKE THE ORNERIEST OLD RIP THAT EVER WAS BUT NOW WHEN HE'D TAKE OFF HIS NEW WHITE BEAVER AND MAKE A BOW AND DO A SMILE HE LOOKED THAT GRAND AND GOOD AND PIOUS THAT YOU'D SAY HE HAD WALKED RIGHT OUT OF THE ARK AND MAYBE WAS OLD LEVITICUS HIMSELF
hypothesis: Why, before he looked like the orne old rip that ever was, but now when he'd take off his new white beaver and make a bow and do a smile he looked that grand and good and pious that you'd say he had walked right out of the ark and maybe was old Leviticus himself.

reference: JIM CLEANED UP THE CANOE AND I GOT MY PADDLE READY
hypothesis: Jim cleaned up the canoe and I got my paddle ready.

reference: WHER YOU BOUND FOR YOUNG MAN
hypothesis: Where are you bound for, young man?

reference: GIT ABOARD SAYS THE KING
hypothesis: Get aboard, says the king.

reference: IF WE'RE LAYING OUT A GARDEN PLANNING ONE BEFORE THE HOUSE YOU KNOW AND THERE YOU'VE A TREE THAT'S STOOD FOR CENTURIES IN THE VERY SPOT OLD AND GNARLED IT MAY BE AND YET YOU DON'T CUT DOWN THE OLD FELLOW TO MAKE ROOM FOR THE FLOWERBEDS BUT LAY OUT YOUR BEDS SO AS TO TAKE ADVANTAGE OF THE TREE
hypothesis: If we are laying out a garden, planning one before the house, you know, and there you have a tree that stood for centuries in the very spot, old and gnarled it may be, and yet you don't cut down the old fellow to make room for the flower beds, but lay out your beds so as to take advantage of the tree.

reference: WELL AND HOW IS YOUR LAND DOING
hypothesis: Well, and how is your land doing?

reference: BUT ONE'S WORK IS THROWN IN FOR NOTHING
hypothesis: But one's work is thrown in for nothing.

reference: OH WELL ONE DOES IT WHAT WOULD YOU HAVE
hypothesis: Oh, well one does it. What would you have?

reference: AND WHAT'S MORE THE LANDOWNER WENT ON LEANING HIS ELBOWS ON THE WINDOW AND CHATTING ON MY SON I MUST TELL YOU HAS NO TASTE FOR IT
hypothesis: And what's more, the landowner went on leaning his elbows on the window and chatting on.

reference: SO THERE'LL BE NO ONE TO KEEP IT UP AND YET ONE DOES IT
hypothesis: So there will be no one to keep it up and yet one does it.

reference: WE WALKED ABOUT THE FIELDS AND THE GARDEN NO SAID HE STEPAN VASSILIEVITCH EVERYTHING'S WELL LOOKED AFTER BUT YOUR GARDEN'S NEGLECTED
hypothesis: We walked about the fields and the garden. No, said he, Stepan Malevich, everything's well looked after, but your garden's neglected.

reference: TO MY THINKING I'D CUT DOWN THAT LIME TREE
hypothesis: To my thinking, I'd cut down the lime tree.

reference: HERE YOU'VE THOUSANDS OF LIMES AND EACH WOULD MAKE TWO GOOD BUNDLES OF BARK
hypothesis: Here you have thousands of limes and each would make two good bundles of bark.

reference: YOU'RE MARRIED I'VE HEARD SAID THE LANDOWNER
hypothesis: You're married, I heard, said the landowner.

reference: YES IT'S RATHER STRANGE HE WENT ON
hypothesis: Yes, it's rather strange, he went on.

reference: THE LANDOWNER CHUCKLED UNDER HIS WHITE MUSTACHES
hypothesis: The landowner chuckled under his white moustaches.

reference: WHY DON'T WE CUT DOWN OUR PARKS FOR TIMBER
hypothesis: Why don't we cut down our parks for timber?

reference: SAID LEVIN RETURNING TO A THOUGHT THAT HAD STRUCK HIM
hypothesis: Said Levin, returning to a thought that had struck him.

reference: THERE'S A CLASS INSTINCT TOO OF WHAT ONE OUGHT AND OUGHTN'T TO DO
hypothesis: There is a class instinct too of what one ought and ought not to do.

reference: THERE'S THE PEASANTS TOO I WONDER AT THEM SOMETIMES ANY GOOD PEASANT TRIES TO TAKE ALL THE LAND HE CAN
hypothesis: There's the peasants too. I wonder at them sometimes.

reference: WITHOUT A RETURN TOO AT A SIMPLE LOSS
hypothesis: Without a return to add a simple loss.

reference: I SWEAR IT ANSWERED SANCHO
hypothesis: I swear, answered Sancho.

reference: I SAY SO CONTINUED DON QUIXOTE BECAUSE I HATE TAKING AWAY ANYONE'S GOOD NAME
hypothesis: I say so, continued Don Quixote, because I hate taking away anyone's good name.

reference: I SAY REPLIED SANCHO THAT I SWEAR TO HOLD MY TONGUE ABOUT IT TILL THE END OF YOUR WORSHIP'S DAYS AND GOD GRANT I MAY BE ABLE TO LET IT OUT TOMORROW
hypothesis: I say, replied Sancho, that I swear to hold my tongue about it till the end of your worship days, and God grant I may be able to let it out tomorrow.

reference: THOUGH YOUR WORSHIP WAS NOT SO BADLY OFF HAVING IN YOUR ARMS THAT INCOMPARABLE BEAUTY YOU SPOKE OF BUT I WHAT DID I HAVE EXCEPT THE HEAVIEST WHACKS I THINK I HAD IN ALL MY LIFE
hypothesis: Though your worship was not so badly off having in your arms the incomparable beauty you spoke of. But I, what did I have except the heaviest wax that I think I had in all my life?

reference: UNLUCKY ME AND THE MOTHER THAT BORE ME
hypothesis: Unlucky me into the mother that bore me.

reference: DIDN'T I SAY SO WORSE LUCK TO MY LINE SAID SANCHO
hypothesis: Didn't I say so? Worst luck to my line, said Sancho.

reference: IT CANNOT BE THE MOOR ANSWERED DON QUIXOTE FOR THOSE UNDER ENCHANTMENT DO NOT LET THEMSELVES BE SEEN BY ANYONE
hypothesis: It cannot be the more, answered Don Quixote, for those under enchantment do not let themselves be seen by anyone.

reference: IF THEY DON'T LET THEMSELVES BE SEEN THEY LET THEMSELVES BE FELT SAID SANCHO IF NOT LET MY SHOULDERS SPEAK TO THE POINT
hypothesis: If they do not let themselves be seen, they let themselves be felt, said Sancho. if not, let my shoulders speak to the point.

reference: MINE COULD SPEAK TOO SAID DON QUIXOTE BUT THAT IS NOT A SUFFICIENT REASON FOR BELIEVING THAT WHAT WE SEE IS THE ENCHANTED MOOR
hypothesis: Mine could speak too, said Don Quixote, but that is not a sufficient reason for believing that what we see is the enchanted Moor.

reference: THE OFFICER TURNED TO HIM AND SAID WELL HOW GOES IT GOOD MAN
hypothesis: The officer turned to him and said, well, how goes it, good man?

reference: SANCHO GOT UP WITH PAIN ENOUGH IN HIS BONES AND WENT AFTER THE INNKEEPER IN THE DARK AND MEETING THE OFFICER WHO WAS LOOKING TO SEE WHAT HAD BECOME OF HIS ENEMY HE SAID TO HIM SENOR WHOEVER YOU ARE DO US THE FAVOUR AND KINDNESS TO GIVE US A LITTLE ROSEMARY OIL SALT AND WINE FOR IT IS WANTED TO CURE ONE OF THE BEST KNIGHTS ERRANT ON EARTH WHO LIES ON YONDER BED WOUNDED BY THE HANDS OF THE ENCHANTED MOOR THAT IS IN THIS INN
hypothesis: Sancho got up with pain enough in his bones and went after the innkeeper in the dark, and meeting the officer who was looking to see what had become of his enemy, he said to him, Signor, whoever you are, do us the favor and kindness to give us a little rosemary, oil, salt and wine, for it is wanted to cure one of our best knights errant on earth, who lies on yonder bed wounded by the hands of the enchanted Moor that is in this inn.

reference: TO BE BRIEF HE TOOK THE MATERIALS OF WHICH HE MADE A COMPOUND MIXING THEM ALL AND BOILING THEM A GOOD WHILE UNTIL IT SEEMED TO HIM THEY HAD COME TO PERFECTION
hypothesis: To be brief, he took the materials of which he made a compound, mixing them all and boiling them.

reference: SANCHO PANZA WHO ALSO REGARDED THE AMENDMENT OF HIS MASTER AS MIRACULOUS BEGGED HIM TO GIVE HIM WHAT WAS LEFT IN THE PIGSKIN WHICH WAS NO SMALL QUANTITY
hypothesis: Sancho Panza, who also regarded the amendment of his master as miraculous, begged him to give him what was left in the pigskin, which was no small quantity.

reference: DON QUIXOTE CONSENTED AND HE TAKING IT WITH BOTH HANDS IN GOOD FAITH AND WITH A BETTER WILL GULPED DOWN AND DRAINED OFF VERY LITTLE LESS THAN HIS MASTER
hypothesis: Don Quixote consented, and he, taking it with both hands in good faith and with a better will, gulped it down and drained off very little less than his master.

reference: IF YOUR WORSHIP KNEW THAT RETURNED SANCHO WOE BETIDE ME AND ALL MY KINDRED WHY DID YOU LET ME TASTE IT
hypothesis: If your worship knew that, returned Sancho, woe betide me and all my kindred. Why did you let me taste it?

reference: SEARCH YOUR MEMORY AND IF YOU FIND ANYTHING OF THIS KIND YOU NEED ONLY TELL ME OF IT AND I PROMISE YOU BY THE ORDER OF KNIGHTHOOD WHICH I HAVE RECEIVED TO PROCURE YOU SATISFACTION AND REPARATION TO THE UTMOST OF YOUR DESIRE
hypothesis: Search your memory and if you find anything of this kind you need only tell me of it, and I promise you by the order of knighthood which I have received to procure you satisfaction and reparation to the utmost of your desire.

reference: THEN THIS IS AN INN SAID DON QUIXOTE
hypothesis: Then this is an inn, said Don Quixote.

reference: AND A VERY RESPECTABLE ONE SAID THE INNKEEPER
hypothesis: And a very respectable one, said the innkeeper.

reference: THE CRIES OF THE POOR BLANKETED WRETCH WERE SO LOUD THAT THEY REACHED THE EARS OF HIS MASTER WHO HALTING TO LISTEN ATTENTIVELY WAS PERSUADED THAT SOME NEW ADVENTURE WAS COMING UNTIL HE CLEARLY PERCEIVED THAT IT WAS HIS SQUIRE WHO UTTERED THEM
hypothesis: The cries of the poor blanketed wretch were so loud that they reached the ears of his master, who, halting to listen attentively, was persuaded that some new adventure was coming, until he clearly perceived that it was his squire who uttered them.

reference: HE SAW HIM RISING AND FALLING IN THE AIR WITH SUCH GRACE AND NIMBLENESS THAT HAD HIS RAGE ALLOWED HIM IT IS MY BELIEF HE WOULD HAVE LAUGHED
hypothesis: He saw him rising and falling in the air with such grace and nimbleness that had his rage allowed him, it is my belief he would have laughed.

reference: SANCHO TOOK IT AND AS HE WAS RAISING IT TO HIS MOUTH HE WAS STOPPED BY THE CRIES OF HIS MASTER EXCLAIMING SANCHO MY SON DRINK NOT WATER DRINK IT NOT MY SON FOR IT WILL KILL THEE SEE HERE I HAVE THE BLESSED BALSAM AND HE HELD UP THE FLASK OF LIQUOR AND WITH DRINKING TWO DROPS OF IT THOU WILT CERTAINLY BE RESTORED
hypothesis: Sancho took it, and as he was raising it to his mouth, he was stopped by the cries of his master, exclaiming, Sancho, my son, drink not water. Drink it not, my son, for it will kill thee. See here I have the blessed balsam, and he held up the flask of liquor, and with drinking two drops of it thou wilt certainly be restored.

reference: LOBSTERS AND LOBSTERS
hypothesis: Lobsters and lobsters

reference: WHEN IS A LOBSTER NOT A LOBSTER WHEN IT IS A CRAYFISH
hypothesis: When is a lobster not a lobster? When it is a crayfish?

reference: THIS QUESTION AND ANSWER MIGHT WELL GO INTO THE PRIMER OF INFORMATION FOR THOSE WHO COME TO SAN FRANCISCO FROM THE EAST FOR WHAT IS CALLED A LOBSTER IN SAN FRANCISCO IS NOT A LOBSTER AT ALL BUT A CRAYFISH
hypothesis: This question and answer might well go into the primer of information for those who come to San Francisco from the East. For what is called a lobster in San Francisco is not a lobster at all, but a crayfish.

reference: THE PACIFIC CRAYFISH HOWEVER SERVES EVERY PURPOSE AND WHILE MANY CONTEND THAT ITS MEAT IS NOT SO DELICATE IN FLAVOR AS THAT OF ITS EASTERN COUSIN THE CALIFORNIAN WILL AS STRENUOUSLY INSIST THAT IT IS BETTER BUT OF COURSE SOMETHING MUST ALWAYS BE ALLOWED FOR THE PATRIOTISM OF THE CALIFORNIAN
hypothesis: The Pacific crayfish, however, serves every purpose, and while many contend that its meat is not so delicate in flavor as that of its Eastern cousin, the Californian will as strenuously insist that it is better. But of course something must always be allowed for the patriotism of the Californian.

reference: A BOOK COULD BE WRITTEN ABOUT THIS RESTAURANT AND THEN ALL WOULD NOT BE TOLD FOR ALL ITS SECRETS CAN NEVER BE KNOWN
hypothesis: A book could be written about this restaurant and then all would not be told for all its secrets can never be known.

reference: IT WAS HERE THAT MOST MAGNIFICENT DINNERS WERE ARRANGED IT WAS HERE THAT EXTRAORDINARY DISHES WERE CONCOCTED BY CHEFS OF WORLD WIDE FAME IT WAS HERE THAT LOBSTER A LA NEWBERG REACHED ITS HIGHEST PERFECTION AND THIS IS THE RECIPE THAT WAS FOLLOWED WHEN IT WAS PREPARED IN THE DELMONICO
hypothesis: It was here that most magnificent dinners were arranged. It was here that extraordinary dishes were concocted by chefs of worldwide fame. It was here that lobster a la Neuburg reached its highest perfection. And this is the recipe that was followed when it was prepared in the Do Moni Go.

reference: LOBSTER A LA NEWBERG
hypothesis: Lobster Owen Newberg.

reference: ONE POUND OF LOBSTER MEAT ONE TEASPOONFUL OF BUTTER ONE HALF PINT OF CREAM YOLKS OF FOUR EGGS ONE WINE GLASS OF SHERRY LOBSTER FAT
hypothesis: One pound of lobster meat, one teaspoonful of butter, one half pint of cream, yolks of four eggs, one wine glass of sherry lobster fat.

reference: PUT THIS IN A DOUBLE BOILER AND LET COOK UNTIL THICK STIRRING CONSTANTLY
hypothesis: Put this in a double boiler and let cook until thick, stirring constantly.

reference: SERVE IN A CHAFING DISH WITH THIN SLICES OF DRY TOAST
hypothesis: Serve in a chafing dish with thin slices of dry toast.

reference: KING OF SHELL FISH
hypothesis: King of Shellfish.

reference: ONE HAS TO COME TO SAN FRANCISCO TO PARTAKE OF THE KING OF SHELL FISH THE MAMMOTH PACIFIC CRAB
hypothesis: One has to come to San Francisco to partake of the king of shellfish, the mammoth Pacific crab.

reference: I SAY COME TO SAN FRANCISCO ADVISEDLY FOR WHILE THE CRAB IS FOUND ALL ALONG THE COAST IT IS PREPARED NOWHERE SO DELICIOUSLY AS IN SAN FRANCISCO
hypothesis: I say come to San Francisco advisedly, for while the crab is found all along the coast, it is prepared nowhere so deliciously as in San Francisco.

reference: GOBEY'S PASSED WITH THE FIRE AND THE LITTLE RESTAURANT BEARING HIS NAME AND IN CHARGE OF HIS WIDOW IN UNION SQUARE AVENUE HAS NOT ATTAINED THE FAME OF THE OLD PLACE
hypothesis: Goby Pass with the fire and the little restaurant bearing his name and in charge of his widow in Union Square Avenue has not attained the fame of the old place.

reference: IT IS POSSIBLE THAT SHE KNOWS THE SECRET OF PREPARING CRAB AS IT WAS PREPARED IN THE GOBEY'S OF BEFORE THE FIRE BUT HIS PRESTIGE DID NOT DESCEND TO HER
hypothesis: It is possible that she knows the secret of preparing crab as it was prepared in the gobies of before the fire, but his prestige did not descend to her.

reference: GOBEY'S CRAB STEW
hypothesis: Goby crab stew.

reference: TAKE THE MEAT OF ONE LARGE CRAB SCRAPING OUT ALL OF THE FAT FROM THE SHELL
hypothesis: Take the meat of one large crab, scraping out all the fat from the shell.

reference: SOAK THE CRAB MEAT IN THE SHERRY TWO HOURS BEFORE COOKING
hypothesis: Soak the crab meat in the sherry 2 h before cooking.

reference: CHOP FINE THE ONION SWEET PEPPER AND TOMATO WITH THE ROSEMARY
hypothesis: Chop fine the onion, sweet pepper and tomato with the rosemary.

reference: HEAT THIS IN A STEWPAN AND WHEN SIMMERING ADD THE SHERRY AND CRAB MEAT AND LET ALL COOK TOGETHER WITH A SLOW FIRE FOR EIGHT MINUTES
hypothesis: Heat this in a stew pan and when simmering add the sherry and crab meat and let all cook together with a slow fire for 8 min.

reference: SERVE IN A CHAFING DISH WITH TOASTED CRACKERS OR THIN SLICES OF TOASTED BREAD
hypothesis: Serve in a chafing dish with toasted crackers or thin slices of toasted bread.

reference: LOBSTER IN MINIATURE
hypothesis: Lobster in miniature.

reference: SO FAR IT HAS BEEN USED MOSTLY FOR GARNISHMENT OF OTHER DISHES AND IT IS ONLY RECENTLY THAT THE HOF BRAU HAS BEEN MAKING A SPECIALTY OF THEM
hypothesis: So far it has been used mostly for garnishment of other dishes and it is only recently that the Hof brow has been making a specialty of them.

reference: ALL OF THE BETTER CLASS RESTAURANTS HOWEVER WILL SERVE THEM IF YOU ORDER THEM
hypothesis: All of the better class restaurants, however, will serve them if you order them.

reference: THIS IS THE RECIPE FOR EIGHT PEOPLE AND IT IS WELL WORTH TRYING IF YOU ARE GIVING A DINNER OF IMPORTANCE
hypothesis: This is the recipe for eight people and it is well worth trying if you are giving a dinner of importance.

reference: BISQUE OF CRAWFISH
hypothesis: Disc of crawfish

reference: TAKE THIRTY CRAWFISH FROM WHICH REMOVE THE GUT CONTAINING THE GALL IN THE FOLLOWING MANNER TAKE FIRM HOLD OF THE CRAWFISH WITH THE LEFT HAND SO AS TO AVOID BEING PINCHED BY ITS CLAWS WITH THE THUMB AND FOREFINGER OF THE RIGHT HAND PINCH THE EXTREME END OF THE CENTRAL FIN OF THE TAIL AND WITH A SUDDEN JERK THE GUT WILL BE WITHDRAWN
hypothesis: Take 30 crawfish, from which remove the gut containing the gall in the following manner Take firm hold of the crawfish with the left hand so as to avoid being pinched by its claws. With the thumb and forefinger of the right hand pinch the extreme end of the central fin of the tail, and with a sudden jerk the gut will be withdrawn.

reference: MINCE OR CUT INTO SMALL DICE A CARROT AN ONION ONE HEAD OF CELERY AND A FEW PARSLEY ROOTS AND TO THESE ADD A BAY LEAF A SPRIG OF THYME A LITTLE MINIONETTE PEPPER AND TWO OUNCES OF BUTTER
hypothesis: Mince or cut into small dice a carrot, an onion, one head of celery, and a few parsley roots, and to these add a bay leaf, a sprig of thyme, a little mino nut pepper, and 2 oz. of butter.

reference: PUT THESE INGREDIENTS INTO A STEWPAN AND FRY THEM TEN MINUTES THEN THROW IN THE CRAWFISH AND POUR ON THEM HALF A BOTTLE OF FRENCH WHITE WINE
hypothesis: Put these ingredients into a stew pan and fry them 10 min. Then throw in the crawfish and pour on them half a bottle of French white wine.

reference: ALLOW THIS TO BOIL AND THEN ADD A QUART OF STRONG CONSOMME AND LET ALL CONTINUE BOILING FOR HALF AN HOUR
hypothesis: Allow this to boil and then add a quart of strong cons and let all continue boiling for half an hour.

reference: PICK OUT THE CRAWFISH AND STRAIN THE BROTH THROUGH A NAPKIN BY PRESSURE INTO A BASIN IN ORDER TO EXTRACT ALL THE ESSENCE FROM THE VEGETABLES
hypothesis: Pick out the crawfish and strain the broth through a napkin by pressure into a basin in order to extract all the essence from the vegetables.

reference: PICK THE SHELLS OFF TWENTY FIVE OF THE CRAWFISH TAILS TRIM THEM NEATLY AND SET THEM ASIDE UNTIL WANTED
hypothesis: Pick the shells off 25 of the crawfish tails, trim them neatly and set them aside until wanted.

reference: RESERVE SOME OF THE SPAWN ALSO HALF OF THE BODY SHELLS WITH WHICH TO MAKE THE CRAWFISH BUTTER TO FINISH THE SOUP
hypothesis: Reserve some of the spawn, also half of the body shells with which to make the crawfish butter to finish the soup.

reference: THIS BUTTER IS MADE AS FOLLOWS PLACE THE SHELLS ON A BAKING SHEET IN THE OVEN TO DRY LET THE SHELLS COOL AND THEN POUND THEM IN A MORTAR WITH A LITTLE LOBSTER CORAL AND FOUR OUNCES OF FRESH BUTTER THOROUGHLY BRUISING THE WHOLE TOGETHER SO AS TO MAKE A FINE PASTE
hypothesis: This butter is made as follows Place the shells on a baking sheet in the oven to dry. Let the shells cool, and then pound them in a mortar with a little lobster coral and 4 oz. of fresh butter, thoroughly bruising the whole together so as to make a fine paste.

reference: THERE WAS ONCE ON A TIME A WIDOWER WHO HAD A SON AND A DAUGHTER BY HIS FIRST WIFE
hypothesis: There was once on a time a widower who had a son and a daughter by his 1st wife.

reference: FROM THE VERY DAY THAT THE NEW WIFE CAME INTO THE HOUSE THERE WAS NO PEACE FOR THE MAN'S CHILDREN AND NOT A CORNER TO BE FOUND WHERE THEY COULD GET ANY REST SO THE BOY THOUGHT THAT THE BEST THING HE COULD DO WAS TO GO OUT INTO THE WORLD AND TRY TO EARN HIS OWN BREAD
hypothesis: From the very day that the new wife came into the house, there was no peace for the man's children and not a corner to be found where they could get any rest.

reference: BUT HIS SISTER WHO WAS STILL AT HOME FARED WORSE AND WORSE
hypothesis: But his sister, who was still at home, fared worse and worse.

reference: KISS ME GIRL SAID THE HEAD
hypothesis: Kiss me, girl, said the head.

reference: WHEN THE KING ENTERED AND SAW IT HE STOOD STILL AS IF HE WERE IN FETTERS AND COULD NOT STIR FROM THE SPOT FOR THE PICTURE SEEMED TO HIM SO BEAUTIFUL
hypothesis: When the king entered and saw it, he stood still as if he were in fetters, and could not stir from the spot, for the picture seemed to him so beautiful.

reference: THE YOUTH PROMISED TO MAKE ALL THE HASTE HE COULD AND SET FORTH FROM THE KING'S PALACE
hypothesis: You promised to make all the haste he could and set forth from the king's palace.

reference: AT LAST THEY CAME IN SIGHT OF LAND
hypothesis: At last they came in sight of land.

reference: WELL IF MY BROTHER SAYS SO I MUST DO IT SAID THE MAN'S DAUGHTER AND SHE FLUNG HER CASKET INTO THE SEA
hypothesis: Well, if my brother says so, I must do it, said the man's daughter, and she flung her casket into the sea.

reference: WHAT IS MY BROTHER SAYING ASKED HIS SISTER AGAIN
hypothesis: What is my brother saying? asked his sister again.

reference: ON THE FIRST THURSDAY NIGHT AFTER THIS A BEAUTIFUL MAIDEN CAME INTO THE KITCHEN OF THE PALACE AND BEGGED THE KITCHEN MAID WHO SLEPT THERE TO LEND HER A BRUSH
hypothesis: On the 1st Thursday night after this, a beautiful maiden came into the kitchen of the palace and begged the kitchen maid who slept there to lend her a brush.

reference: SHE BEGGED VERY PRETTILY AND GOT IT AND THEN SHE BRUSHED HER HAIR AND THE GOLD DROPPED FROM IT
hypothesis: She begged very prettily and got it and then she brushed her hair and the gold dropped from it.

reference: OUT ON THEE UGLY BUSHY BRIDE SLEEPING SO SOFT BY THE YOUNG KING'S SIDE ON SAND AND STONES MY BED I MAKE AND MY BROTHER SLEEPS WITH THE COLD SNAKE UNPITIED AND UNWEPT
hypothesis: Out on the ugly bushy bride, Sleeping so soft by the young king's side On sand and stones my bed I make, And my brother sleeps with the cold snake unpitied and unwept.

reference: I SHALL COME TWICE MORE AND THEN NEVER AGAIN SAID SHE
hypothesis: I shall come twice more and then never again, said she.

reference: THIS TIME ALSO AS BEFORE SHE BORROWED A BRUSH AND BRUSHED HER HAIR WITH IT AND THE GOLD DROPPED DOWN AS SHE DID IT AND AGAIN SHE SENT THE DOG OUT THREE TIMES AND WHEN DAY DAWNED SHE DEPARTED BUT AS SHE WAS GOING SHE SAID AS SHE HAD SAID BEFORE I SHALL COME ONCE MORE AND THEN NEVER AGAIN
hypothesis: This time also, as before, she borrowed a brush and brushed her hair with it and the gold dropped down as she did it. And again she sent the dog out three times. and when day dawned she departed. But as she was going, she said, as she had said before, I shall come once more and then never again.

reference: NO ONE CAN TELL HOW DELIGHTED THE KING WAS TO GET RID OF THAT HIDEOUS BUSHY BRIDE AND GET A QUEEN WHO WAS BRIGHT AND BEAUTIFUL AS DAY ITSELF
hypothesis: No one can tell how delighted the king was to get rid of that hideous bushy bride and get a queen who was bright and beautiful as day itself.

reference: WILT THOU SERVE ME AND WATCH MY SEVEN FOALS ASKED THE KING
hypothesis: Wilt thou serve me and watch my seven folds? asked the king.

reference: THE YOUTH THOUGHT THAT IT WAS VERY EASY WORK TO WATCH THE FOALS AND THAT HE COULD DO IT WELL ENOUGH
hypothesis: The youth thought that it was very easy work to wash the foals and that he could do it well enough.

reference: HAST THOU WATCHED FAITHFULLY AND WELL THE WHOLE DAY LONG SAID THE KING WHEN THE LAD CAME INTO HIS PRESENCE IN THE EVENING
hypothesis: Hast thou watched faithfully and well the whole day long? said the king, when the lad came into his presence in the evening.

reference: YES THAT I HAVE SAID THE YOUTH
hypothesis: Yes, that I have, said the youth.

reference: HE HAD GONE OUT ONCE TO SEEK A PLACE HE SAID BUT NEVER WOULD HE DO SUCH A THING AGAIN
hypothesis: He had gone out once to seek a place, he said, but never would he do such a thing again.

reference: THEN THE KING PROMISED HIM THE SAME PUNISHMENT AND THE SAME REWARD THAT HE HAD PROMISED HIS BROTHER
hypothesis: Then the king promised him the same punishment and the same reward that he had promised his brother.

reference: WHEN HE HAD RUN AFTER THE FOALS FOR A LONG LONG TIME AND WAS HOT AND TIRED HE PASSED BY A CLEFT IN THE ROCK WHERE AN OLD WOMAN WAS SITTING SPINNING WITH A DISTAFF AND SHE CALLED TO HIM
hypothesis: When he had run after the fools for a long, long time and was hot and tired, he passed by a cleft in the rock where an old woman was sitting spinning with a distaff and she called to him.

reference: COME HITHER COME HITHER MY HANDSOME SON AND LET ME COMB YOUR HAIR
hypothesis: Come hither, come hither, my handsome son, and let me comb your hair.

reference: THE YOUTH LIKED THE THOUGHT OF THIS LET THE FOALS RUN WHERE THEY CHOSE AND SEATED HIMSELF IN THE CLEFT OF THE ROCK BY THE SIDE OF THE OLD HAG
hypothesis: The youth liked the thought of this, let the foals run where they chose, and seated himself in the cleft of the rock by the side of the old hag.

reference: SO THERE HE SAT WITH HIS HEAD ON HER LAP TAKING HIS EASE THE LIVELONG DAY
hypothesis: So there he sat with his head on her lap, taking his ease the livelong day.

reference: ON THE THIRD DAY CINDERLAD WANTED TO SET OUT
hypothesis: On the 3rd day, Cinderlad wanted to set out.

reference: THE TWO BROTHERS LAUGHED AT HIM AND HIS FATHER AND MOTHER BEGGED HIM NOT TO GO BUT ALL TO NO PURPOSE AND CINDERLAD SET OUT ON HIS WAY
hypothesis: The two brothers laughed at him and his father and mother begged him not to go, but all to no purpose. and Cinderlad set out on his way.

reference: I AM WALKING ABOUT IN SEARCH OF A PLACE SAID CINDERLAD
hypothesis: I am walking about in search of a place, said Cinderlad.

reference: I WOULD MUCH RATHER HAVE THE PRINCESS SAID CINDERLAD
hypothesis: I would much rather have the princess said Cinderlad.

reference: AND THUS THEY JOURNEYED ONWARDS A LONG LONG WAY
hypothesis: And thus they journeyed onwards a long, long way.

reference: WHEN THEY HAD GONE THUS FOR A LONG LONG WAY THE FOAL AGAIN ASKED DOST THOU SEE ANYTHING NOW
hypothesis: When they had gone thus for a long, long way, the fool again asks, Dost thou see anything now?

reference: YES NOW I SEE SOMETHING THAT IS WHITE SAID CINDERLAD
hypothesis: Yes, now I see something that is white, said Cinderlad.

reference: IT LOOKS LIKE THE TRUNK OF A GREAT THICK BIRCH TREE
hypothesis: It looks like the trunk of a great thick birch tree.

reference: CINDERLAD TRIED BUT COULD NOT DO IT SO HE HAD TO TAKE A DRAUGHT FROM THE PITCHER AND THEN ONE MORE AND AFTER THAT STILL ANOTHER AND THEN HE WAS ABLE TO WIELD THE SWORD WITH PERFECT EASE
hypothesis: Cinderlad tried but could not do it, so he had to take a draw from the pitcher and then one more and after that still another and then he was able to wield the sword with perfect ease.

reference: FOR WE ARE BROTHERS OF THE PRINCESS WHOM THOU ART TO HAVE WHEN THOU CANST TELL THE KING WHAT WE EAT AND DRINK BUT THERE IS A MIGHTY TROLL WHO HAS CAST A SPELL OVER US
hypothesis: For we are brothers of the princess whom thou art to have when thou canst tell the king what we eat and drink. But there is a mighty troll who has cast a spell over us.

reference: WHEN THEY HAD TRAVELLED A LONG LONG WAY THE FOAL SAID DOST THOU SEE ANYTHING
hypothesis: When they had traveled a long, long way, the fool said, dost thou see anything?

reference: AND NOW INQUIRED THE FOAL SEEST THOU NOTHING NOW
hypothesis: And now inquire the foul. Seest thou nothing now?

reference: NOW THEN SAID THE FOAL DOST THOU NOT SEE ANYTHING NOW
hypothesis: Now then, said the fool, dost thou not see anything now?

reference: THAT IS A RIVER SAID THE FOAL AND WE HAVE TO CROSS IT
hypothesis: That is a river, said the foal, and we have to cross it.

reference: I HAVE DONE MY BEST REPLIED CINDERLAD
hypothesis: I've done my best, replied Cinderlad.

reference: ONCE UPON A TIME THERE WAS A KING IN THE NORTH WHO HAD WON MANY WARS BUT NOW HE WAS OLD
hypothesis: Once upon a time there was a king in the north who had won many wars, but now he was old.

reference: THE OLD KING WENT OUT AND FOUGHT BRAVELY BUT AT LAST HIS SWORD BROKE AND HE WAS WOUNDED AND HIS MEN FLED
