reference: WHY HE JES STRETCH HIS FOOL HAID AS FAR DOWN THAT CHIMNEY AS HE CAN AN LISTEN AN LISTEN
hypothesis: Why, he just stretched his full head as far down the chimney as he can and listen and listen.

reference: BUT HE DON'T MIND THAT
hypothesis: But he don't mind that.

reference: WILL YO' ALLS PLEASE SPEAK A LIL LOUDER HE HOLLER DOWN THE CHIMNEY JES LIKE THAT
hypothesis: Will you all please speak a little louder? He holler down the chimney just like that.

reference: YES SAH SHE SHO'LY WAS PLUMB SCARED
hypothesis: Yes, she surely was plum scared.

reference: THEY LIKE TO CHOKE THAT NO COUNT BUZZARD TO DEATH
hypothesis: They like to choke that no C buzzer to death.

reference: WHEN HE GET HOME HE TRY AN TRY TO BRUSH THAT SOOT OFF BUT IT DONE GET INTO THE SKIN AN IT STAY THERE
hypothesis: When he get home he try and try to brush that suit off but it does get into the skin and it stay there.

reference: A LITTLE SIGH OF SATISFACTION WENT AROUND THE CIRCLE OF LISTENERS
hypothesis: A little sigh of satisfaction went round the circle of listeners.

reference: IT WAS JUST AS GOOD AS ONE OF GRANDFATHER FROG'S
hypothesis: It was just as good as one of Grandfather Frog's.

reference: THE DUKE COMES EVERY MORNING THEY WILL TELL HIM WHEN HE COMES THAT I AM ASLEEP AND PERHAPS HE WILL WAIT UNTIL I WAKE
hypothesis: The duke comes every morning. They will tell him when he comes that I am asleep and perhaps he will wait until I awake.

reference: YES BUT IF I SHOULD ALREADY ASK FOR SOMETHING WHAT
hypothesis: Yes.

reference: WELL DO IT FOR ME FOR I SWEAR TO YOU THAT I DON'T LOVE YOU AS THE OTHERS HAVE LOVED YOU
hypothesis: Well, do it for me, for I swear to you that I don't love you as the others have loved you.

reference: THERE ARE BOLTS ON THE DOOR WRETCH
hypothesis: There are bolts in the door, wretch.

reference: I DON'T KNOW HOW IT IS BUT IT SEEMS TO ME AS IF I DO
hypothesis: I don't know how it is, but it seems to me as if I do.

reference: NOW GO I CAN'T KEEP MY EYES OPEN
hypothesis: Now go. I can't keep my eyes open.

reference: IT SEEMED TO ME AS IF THIS SLEEPING CITY BELONGED TO ME I SEARCHED MY MEMORY FOR THE NAMES OF THOSE WHOSE HAPPINESS I HAD ONCE ENVIED AND I COULD NOT RECALL ONE WITHOUT FINDING MYSELF THE HAPPIER
hypothesis: It seems to me as if this sleeping city belongs to me. I searched my memory for the names of those whose happiness I had once envied and I could not recall one without finding myself the happier.

reference: EDUCATION FAMILY FEELING THE SENSE OF DUTY THE FAMILY ARE STRONG SENTINELS BUT THERE ARE NO SENTINELS SO VIGILANT AS NOT TO BE DECEIVED BY A GIRL OF SIXTEEN TO WHOM NATURE BY THE VOICE OF THE MAN SHE LOVES GIVES THE FIRST COUNSELS OF LOVE ALL THE MORE ARDENT BECAUSE THEY SEEM SO PURE
hypothesis: Education

reference: THE MORE A GIRL BELIEVES IN GOODNESS THE MORE EASILY WILL SHE GIVE WAY IF NOT TO HER LOVER AT LEAST TO LOVE FOR BEING WITHOUT MISTRUST SHE IS WITHOUT FORCE AND TO WIN HER LOVE IS A TRIUMPH THAT CAN BE GAINED BY ANY YOUNG MAN OF FIVE AND TWENTY SEE HOW YOUNG GIRLS ARE WATCHED AND GUARDED
hypothesis: The more a girl believes in goodness, the more easily will she give way, if not to her lover, at least to love. For being without mistrust, she is without force. And to win her love is a triumph that can be gained by any young man of five and 20.

reference: THEN HOW SURELY MUST THEY DESIRE THE WORLD WHICH IS HIDDEN FROM THEM HOW SURELY MUST THEY FIND IT TEMPTING HOW SURELY MUST THEY LISTEN TO THE FIRST VOICE WHICH COMES TO TELL ITS SECRETS THROUGH THEIR BARS AND BLESS THE HAND WHICH IS THE FIRST TO RAISE A CORNER OF THE MYSTERIOUS VEIL
hypothesis: Then how surely must they desire the world which is hidden from them? How surely must they find it tempting? How surely must they listen to the 1st voice which comes to tell its secrets through their bars and bless the hand which is the 1st to raise a corner of the mystery veil.

reference: WITH THEM THE BODY HAS WORN OUT THE SOUL THE SENSES HAVE BURNED UP THE HEART DISSIPATION HAS BLUNTED THE FEELINGS
hypothesis: With them, the body has worn out the soul, the senses have burned up the heart, dissipation has blunted the feelings.

reference: THEY LOVE BY PROFESSION AND NOT BY INSTINCT
hypothesis: They love by profession and not by instinct.

reference: WHEN A CREATURE WHO HAS ALL HER PAST TO REPROACH HERSELF WITH IS TAKEN ALL AT ONCE BY A PROFOUND SINCERE IRRESISTIBLE LOVE OF WHICH SHE HAD NEVER FELT HERSELF CAPABLE WHEN SHE HAS CONFESSED HER LOVE HOW ABSOLUTELY THE MAN WHOM SHE LOVES DOMINATES HER
hypothesis: When a creature who has all her past to reproach herself with is taken all at once by a profound, sincere, irresistible love of which she had never felt herself capable.

reference: THEY KNOW NOT WHAT PROOF TO GIVE
hypothesis: They know not what proof to give.

reference: IN ORDER TO DISTURB THE LABOURERS IN THE FIELD WAS ONE DAY DEVOURED BY A WOLF BECAUSE THOSE WHOM HE HAD SO OFTEN DECEIVED NO LONGER BELIEVED IN HIS CRIES FOR HELP
hypothesis: In order to disturb the laborers in the field, was one day devoured by a wolf, because those whom he had so often deceived no longer believed in his cries for help.

reference: IT IS THE SAME WITH THESE UNHAPPY WOMEN WHEN THEY LOVE SERIOUSLY
hypothesis: This is the same with these unhappy women when they love seriously.

reference: BUT WHEN THE MAN WHO INSPIRES THIS REDEEMING LOVE IS GREAT ENOUGH IN SOUL TO RECEIVE IT WITHOUT REMEMBERING THE PAST WHEN HE GIVES HIMSELF UP TO IT WHEN IN SHORT HE LOVES AS HE IS LOVED THIS MAN DRAINS AT ONE DRAUGHT ALL EARTHLY EMOTIONS AND AFTER SUCH A LOVE HIS HEART WILL BE CLOSED TO EVERY OTHER
hypothesis: But when the man who inspires this redeeming love is great enough in soul to receive it without remembering the past, when he gives himself up to it, when, in short, he loves as he is loved, this man drains at one draught all earthly emotions, and after such a love his heart will be closed to every other.

reference: BUT TO RETURN TO THE FIRST DAY OF MY LIAISON
hypothesis: But to return to the 1st day of my liaison.

reference: WHEN I REACHED HOME I WAS IN A STATE OF MAD GAIETY
hypothesis: When I reached home, I was in a state of mad gaiety.

reference: THE WOMAN BECOMES THE MAN'S MISTRESS AND LOVES HIM
hypothesis: The woman becomes the man's mistress and loves him.

reference: HOW WHY
hypothesis: How

reference: MY WHOLE BEING WAS EXALTED INTO JOY AT THE MEMORY OF THE WORDS WE HAD EXCHANGED DURING THAT FIRST NIGHT
hypothesis: My whole being was exalted into joy at the memory of the words we had exchanged during that 1st night.

reference: HERE ARE MY ORDERS TO NIGHT AT THE VAUDEVILLE
hypothesis: Here are my orders tonight at the Vaudeville.

reference: COME DURING THE THIRD ENTR'ACTE
hypothesis: Come during the 3rd

reference: THE BOXES FILLED ONE AFTER ANOTHER
hypothesis: The boxes filled one after another.

reference: ONLY ONE REMAINED EMPTY THE STAGE BOX
hypothesis: Only one remains empty, the stage box.

reference: AT THE BEGINNING OF THE THIRD ACT I HEARD THE DOOR OF THE BOX ON WHICH MY EYES HAD BEEN ALMOST CONSTANTLY FIXED OPEN AND MARGUERITE APPEARED
hypothesis: At the beginning of the 3rd act, I heard the door of the box on which my eyes had been almost constantly fixed open and Marguerite appeared.

reference: DID SHE LOVE ME ENOUGH TO BELIEVE THAT THE MORE BEAUTIFUL SHE LOOKED THE HAPPIER I SHOULD BE
hypothesis: Did she love me enough to believe that the more beautiful she looks, the happier I should be?

reference: WHAT IS THE MATTER WITH YOU TO NIGHT SAID MARGUERITE RISING AND COMING TO THE BACK OF THE BOX AND KISSING ME ON THE FOREHEAD
hypothesis: What is the matter with you to night? said Marguerite, rising and coming to the back of the box and kissing me on the forehead.

reference: YOU SHOULD GO TO BED SHE REPLIED WITH THAT IRONICAL AIR WHICH WENT SO WELL WITH HER DELICATE AND WITTY FACE
hypothesis: You should go to bed, she replied with that ironic air which went so well with her delicate and witty face.

reference: WHERE AT HOME
hypothesis: Where

reference: YOU STILL LOVE ME CAN YOU ASK
hypothesis: You still love me. Can you ask?

reference: BECAUSE YOU DON'T LIKE SEEING HIM
hypothesis: Because you don't like seeing him.

reference: NONETHELESS I WAS VERY UNHAPPY ALL THE REST OF THE EVENING AND WENT AWAY VERY SADLY AFTER HAVING SEEN PRUDENCE THE COUNT AND MARGUERITE GET INTO THE CARRIAGE WHICH WAS WAITING FOR THEM AT THE DOOR
hypothesis: Nonetheless, I was very unhappy all the rest of the evening and went away very sadly after having seen Prudence, the count and Marguerite get into the carriage which was waiting for them at the door.

reference: I HAVE NOT COME TO HINDER YOU FROM LEAVING PARIS
hypothesis: I have not come to hinder you from leaving Paris.

reference: YOU IN THE WAY MARGUERITE BUT HOW
hypothesis: You in the way, Marguerite, But how

reference: WELL YOU MIGHT HAVE HAD A WOMAN HERE SAID PRUDENCE AND IT WOULD HARDLY HAVE BEEN AMUSING FOR HER TO SEE TWO MORE ARRIVE
hypothesis: Well, you might have had a woman here, said Prudence, and it would hardly have been amusing for her to see two more arrive.

reference: DURING THIS REMARK MARGUERITE LOOKED AT ME ATTENTIVELY
hypothesis: During this remark, Marguerite looked at me attentively.

reference: MY DEAR PRUDENCE I ANSWERED YOU DO NOT KNOW WHAT YOU ARE SAYING
hypothesis: My dear Prudence, I answered, you do not know what you are saying.

reference: YES BUT BESIDES NOT WISHING TO PUT YOU OUT I WAS SURE THAT IF YOU CAME AS FAR AS MY DOOR YOU WOULD WANT TO COME UP AND AS I COULD NOT LET YOU I DID NOT WISH TO LET YOU GO AWAY BLAMING ME FOR SAYING NO
hypothesis: Yes, but besides not wishing to put you out, I was sure that if you came as far as my door, you would want to come up. And as I could not let you, I did not wish to let you go away blaming me for saying no.

reference: BECAUSE I AM WATCHED AND THE LEAST SUSPICION MIGHT DO ME THE GREATEST HARM
hypothesis: Because I am watched and the least suspicion might do me the greatest harm.

reference: IS THAT REALLY THE ONLY REASON
hypothesis: Is that really the only reason?

reference: IF THERE WERE ANY OTHER I WOULD TELL YOU FOR WE ARE NOT TO HAVE ANY SECRETS FROM ONE ANOTHER NOW
hypothesis: If there were any other, I would tell you, for we are not to have any secrets from one another now.

reference: HONESTLY DO YOU CARE FOR ME A LITTLE A GREAT DEAL
hypothesis: Honestly, do you care for me a little?

reference: I FANCIED FOR A MOMENT THAT I MIGHT GIVE MYSELF THAT HAPPINESS FOR SIX MONTHS YOU WOULD NOT HAVE IT YOU INSISTED ON KNOWING THE MEANS
hypothesis: I fancied for a moment that I might give myself that happiness for six months. You would not have it. You insisted on knowing the means.

reference: WELL GOOD HEAVENS THE MEANS WERE EASY ENOUGH TO GUESS
hypothesis: Well, good heavens, the means were easy enough to guess.

reference: I LISTENED AND I GAZED AT MARGUERITE WITH ADMIRATION
hypothesis: I listened and I gazed at Marguerite with admiration.

reference: WHEN I THOUGHT THAT THIS MARVELLOUS CREATURE WHOSE FEET I HAD ONCE LONGED TO KISS WAS WILLING TO LET ME TAKE MY PLACE IN HER THOUGHTS MY PART IN HER LIFE AND THAT I WAS NOT YET CONTENT WITH WHAT SHE GAVE ME I ASKED IF MAN'S DESIRE HAS INDEED LIMITS WHEN SATISFIED AS PROMPTLY AS MINE HAD BEEN IT REACHED AFTER SOMETHING FURTHER
hypothesis: When I thought that this marvelous creature whose feet I had once longed to kiss was willing to let me take my place in her thoughts, my part in her life, and that I was not yet content with what she gave me, I asked if man's desire had indeed limits when satisfied as promptly as mine had been, it reached after something further.

reference: TRULY SHE CONTINUED WE POOR CREATURES OF CHANCE HAVE FANTASTIC DESIRES AND INCONCEIVABLE LOVES
hypothesis: Truly, she continued, we poor creatures of chance have fantastic desire and inconceivable loves.

reference: WE ARE NOT ALLOWED TO HAVE HEARTS UNDER PENALTY OF BEING HOOTED DOWN AND OF RUINING OUR CREDIT
hypothesis: We are not allowed to have hearts under penalty of being hooted down and of ruining our credit.

reference: WE NO LONGER BELONG TO OURSELVES
hypothesis: We no longer belong to ourselves.

reference: WE STAND FIRST IN THEIR SELF ESTEEM LAST IN THEIR ESTEEM
hypothesis: We stand 1st in their self esteem, last in their esteem.

reference: NEVER DO THEY GIVE YOU ADVICE WHICH IS NOT LUCRATIVE
hypothesis: Never do they give you advice which is not lucrative.

reference: IT MEANS LITTLE ENOUGH TO THEM THAT WE SHOULD HAVE TEN LOVERS EXTRA AS LONG AS THEY GET DRESSES OR A BRACELET OUT OF THEM AND THAT THEY CAN DRIVE IN OUR CARRIAGE FROM TIME TO TIME OR COME TO OUR BOX AT THE THEATRE
hypothesis: It means little enough to them that we should have ten lovers extra as long as they get dresses or a bracelet out of them and that they can drive in our carriage from time to time or come to our box at the theatre.

reference: SUCH A MAN I FOUND IN THE DUKE BUT THE DUKE IS OLD AND OLD AGE NEITHER PROTECTS NOR CONSOLES
hypothesis: Such a man I found in the Duke. but Duke is old, and old age neither protects nor consoles.

reference: I THOUGHT I COULD ACCEPT THE LIFE WHICH HE OFFERED ME BUT WHAT WOULD YOU HAVE
hypothesis: I thought I could accept the life which he offered me, but what would you have?

reference: WHAT I LOVED IN YOU WAS NOT THE MAN WHO WAS BUT THE MAN WHO WAS GOING TO BE
hypothesis: What I loved in you was not the man who was, but the man who was going to be.

reference: MARGUERITE TIRED OUT WITH THIS LONG CONFESSION THREW HERSELF BACK ON THE SOFA AND TO STIFLE A SLIGHT COUGH PUT UP HER HANDKERCHIEF TO HER LIPS AND FROM THAT TO HER EYES
hypothesis: Marguerite, tired out with this long confession, threw herself back on the sofa and to stifle a slight cough, put up her handkerchief to her lips and from that to her eyes.

reference: MARGUERITE DO WITH ME AS YOU WILL I AM YOUR SLAVE YOUR DOG BUT IN THE NAME OF HEAVEN TEAR UP THE LETTER WHICH I WROTE TO YOU AND DO NOT MAKE ME LEAVE YOU TO MORROW IT WOULD KILL ME
hypothesis: Marguerite, do with me as you will. I am your slave, your dog, but in the name of heaven, tear up the letter which I wrote to you, and do not make me leave you tomorrow. It would kill me.

reference: MARGUERITE DREW THE LETTER FROM HER BOSOM AND HANDING IT TO ME WITH A SMILE OF INFINITE SWEETNESS SAID
hypothesis: Marguerite drew the letter from her bosom, and handing it to me with a smile of infinite sweetness, said

reference: HERE IT IS I HAVE BROUGHT IT BACK
hypothesis: Here it is. I have brought it back.

reference: I TORE THE LETTER INTO FRAGMENTS AND KISSED WITH TEARS THE HAND THAT GAVE IT TO ME
hypothesis: I tore the letter into fragments and kissed with tears the hand that gave it to me.

reference: LOOK HERE PRUDENCE DO YOU KNOW WHAT HE WANTS SAID MARGUERITE
hypothesis: Look here, Prudence, do you know what he wants? said Marguerite.

reference: HE WANTS YOU TO FORGIVE HIM
hypothesis: He wants you to forgive him.

reference: ONE HAS TO BUT HE WANTS MORE THAN THAT WHAT THEN
hypothesis: One has to, but he wants more than that. What then?

reference: I EMBRACED MARGUERITE UNTIL SHE WAS ALMOST STIFLED
hypothesis: I embraced Marguerite until she was almost stifled.

reference: AS I THOUGHT THAT THIS WAS DUE TO SOME FAULT IN THE EARTH I WANTED TO MAKE THESE FIRST EXPERIMENTS BEFORE I UNDERTOOK MY PERSEUS
hypothesis: As I thought that this was due to some fault in the earth, I wanted to make these 1st experiments before I undertook my Perseus.

reference: WHEN I SAW THAT THIS BUST CAME OUT SHARP AND CLEAN I SET AT ONCE TO CONSTRUCT A LITTLE FURNACE IN THE WORKSHOP ERECTED FOR ME BY THE DUKE AFTER MY OWN PLANS AND DESIGN IN THE HOUSE WHICH THE DUKE HAD GIVEN ME
hypothesis: When I saw that this bust came out sharp and clean, I set at once to construct a little furnace in the workshop erected for me by the Duke after my own plans and design in the house which the Duke had given me.

reference: IT WAS AN EXTREMELY DIFFICULT TASK AND I WAS ANXIOUS TO OBSERVE ALL THE NICETIES OF ART WHICH I HAD LEARNED SO AS NOT TO LAPSE INTO SOME ERROR
hypothesis: It was an extremely difficult task and I was anxious to observe all the niceties of art which I had learned so as not to lapse into some error.

reference: I IN MY TURN FEEL THE SAME DESIRE AND HOPE TO PLAY MY PART LIKE THEM THEREFORE MY LORD GIVE ME THE LEAVE TO GO
hypothesis: I in my turn feel the same desire and hope to play my part like them. Therefore, my lord, give me the leave to go.

reference: BUT BEWARE OF LETTING BANDINELLO QUIT YOU RATHER BESTOW UPON HIM ALWAYS MORE THAN HE DEMANDS FOR IF HE GOES INTO FOREIGN PARTS HIS IGNORANCE IS SO PRESUMPTUOUS THAT HE IS JUST THE MAN TO DISGRACE OUR MOST ILLUSTRIOUS SCHOOL
hypothesis: But beware of letting Ben Nello quit. You rather bestow upon him always more than he demands. For if he goes into foreign parts, his ignorance is so presumptuous that he is just the man to disgrace our most illustrious school.

reference: I ASK NO FURTHER REWARD FOR MY LABOURS UP TO THIS TIME THAN THE GRACIOUS FAVOUR OF YOUR MOST ILLUSTRIOUS EXCELLENCY
hypothesis: I ask no further reward for my labours up to this time than the gracious favour of your Most Illustrious Excellency.

reference: THEN I THANKED HIM AND SAID I HAD NO GREATER DESIRE THAN TO SHOW THOSE ENVIOUS FOLK THAT I HAD IT IN ME TO EXECUTE THE PROMISED WORK
hypothesis: Then I thanked him and said I had no greater desire than to show those envious folk that I had it in me to execute the promised work.

reference: I HAD BETTER LOOK TO MY CONDUCT FOR IT HAD COME TO HIS EARS THAT I RELIED UPON HIS FAVOUR TO TAKE IN FIRST ONE MAN AND THEN ANOTHER
hypothesis: I had better look to my conduct, for it had come to his ears that I relied upon his favour to take in 1st one man and then another.

reference: I BEGGED HIS MOST ILLUSTRIOUS EXCELLENCY TO NAME A SINGLE PERSON WHOM I HAD EVER TAKEN IN
hypothesis: I begged his Most Illustrious Excellency to name a single person whom I had ever taken in.

reference: I SAID MY LORD I THANK YOU AND BEG YOU TO CONDESCEND SO FAR AS TO LISTEN TO FOUR WORDS IT IS TRUE THAT HE LENT ME A PAIR OF OLD SCALES TWO ANVILS AND THREE LITTLE HAMMERS WHICH ARTICLES I BEGGED HIS WORKMAN GIORGIO DA CORTONA FIFTEEN DAYS AGO TO FETCH BACK
hypothesis: I said, my lord, I thank you and beg you to condescend so far as to listen to four words. It is true that he lent me a pair of old scales, two anvils and three little hammers, which articles I begged his workman, Giorgio da Cortona, 15 days ago, to fetch back.

reference: GIORGIO CAME FOR THEM HIMSELF
hypothesis: Giorgio came for them. His

reference: I HOPE TO PROVE ON WHAT ACCOUNT THAT SCOUNDREL TRIES TO BRING ME INTO DISGRACE
hypothesis: I hope to prove on what account that scoundrel tries to bring me into disgrace.

reference: WHEN HE HAD HEARD THIS SPEECH THE DUKE ROSE UP IN ANGER AND SENT FOR BERNARDONE WHO WAS FORCED TO TAKE FLIGHT AS FAR AS VENICE HE AND ANTONIO LANDI WITH HIM
hypothesis: When he had heard this speech, the duke rose up in anger and sent for Bernard, who was forced to take flight as far as Venice.

reference: YOU HAD BETTER PUT THIS TO THE PROOF AND I WILL GO AT ONCE TO THE BARGELLO
hypothesis: You had better put this to the proof and I will go at once to the barge.

reference: I AM WILLING TO ENTER INTO COMPETITION WITH THE ANCIENTS AND FEEL ABLE TO SURPASS THEM FOR SINCE THOSE EARLY DAYS IN WHICH I MADE THE MEDALS OF POPE CLEMENT I HAVE LEARNED SO MUCH THAT I CAN NOW PRODUCE FAR BETTER PIECES OF THE KIND I THINK I CAN ALSO OUTDO THE COINS I STRUCK FOR DUKE ALESSANDRO WHICH ARE STILL HELD IN HIGH ESTEEM IN LIKE MANNER I COULD MAKE FOR YOU LARGE PIECES OF GOLD AND SILVER PLATE AS I DID SO OFTEN FOR THAT NOBLE MONARCH KING FRANCIS OF FRANCE THANKS TO THE GREAT CONVENIENCES HE ALLOWED ME WITHOUT EVER LOSING TIME FOR THE EXECUTION OF COLOSSAL STATUES OR OTHER WORKS OF THE SCULPTORS CRAFT
hypothesis: I am willing to enter into competition with the ancients and feel able to surpass them. for since those early days in which I made the medals of Pope Clement, I have learned so much that I can now produce far better pieces of the kind. I think I can also outdo the coins I struck for Duke Alessandro, which are still held in high esteem. In like manner, I could make for you large pieces of gold and silver plate, as I did so often for that noble monarch, King Francis of France, thanks to the great conveniences he allowed me without ever losing time for the execution of colossal statues or other works of the sculptor's craft.

reference: AFTER SEVERAL MONTHS WERE WASTED AND PIERO WOULD NEITHER WORK NOR PUT MEN TO WORK UPON THE PIECE I MADE HIM GIVE IT BACK
hypothesis: After several months were wasted and Piero would neither work nor put men to work upon the piece, I made him give it back.

reference: AMONG ARTISTS CERTAIN ENRAGED SCULPTORS LAUGHED AT ME AND CALLED ME THE NEW SCULPTOR
hypothesis: Among artists, certain enraged sculptors laughed at me and called me the new sculptor.

reference: NOW I HOPE TO SHOW THEM THAT I AM AN OLD SCULPTOR IF GOD SHALL GRANT ME THE BOON OF FINISHING MY PERSEUS FOR THAT NOBLE PIAZZA OF HIS MOST ILLUSTRIOUS EXCELLENCY
hypothesis: Now I hope to show them that I am an old sculptor, if God shall grant me the boon of finishing my Perseus for that noble piazza of his most illustrious Excellency.

reference: HAVING THIS EXCELLENT RESOLVE IN HEART I REACHED MY HOME
hypothesis: Having this excellent resolve in heart, I reached my home.

reference: BUT THE MIDDLE SON WAS LITTLE AND LORN HE WAS NEITHER DARK NOR FAIR HE WAS NEITHER HANDSOME NOR STRONG
hypothesis: But the middle son was little and lorn. He was neither dark nor fair. He was neither handsome nor strong.

reference: THROWING HIMSELF ON HIS KNEES BEFORE THE KING HE CRIED OH ROYAL SIRE BESTOW UPON ME ALSO A SWORD AND A STEED THAT I MAY UP AND AWAY TO FOLLOW MY BRETHREN
hypothesis: Throwing himself on his knees before the king, he cried, O royal sire, bestow upon me also a sword and a steed, that I may up and away to follow my brethren.

reference: BUT THE KING LAUGHED HIM TO SCORN THOU A SWORD HE QUOTH
hypothesis: But the king laughed him to scorn. Thou a sword? he quoth.

reference: IN SOOTH THOU SHALT HAVE ONE BUT IT SHALL BE ONE BEFITTING THY MAIDEN SIZE AND COURAGE IF SO SMALL A WEAPON CAN BE FOUND IN ALL MY KINGDOM
hypothesis: In sooth thou shalt have one, but it shall be one befitting thy maiden size and courage, if so small a weapon can be found in all my kingdom.

reference: FORTHWITH THE GRINNING JESTER BEGAN SHRIEKING WITH LAUGHTER SO THAT THE BELLS UPON HIS MOTLEY CAP WERE ALL SET A JANGLING
hypothesis: Forthwith the grinning jester began shrieking with laughter, so that the bells upon his motley cap were all set a jangling.

reference: I DID BUT LAUGH TO THINK THE SWORD OF ETHELRIED HAD BEEN SO QUICKLY FOUND RESPONDED THE JESTER AND HE POINTED TO THE SCISSORS HANGING FROM THE TAILOR'S GIRDLE
hypothesis: I did but laugh to think the sword of Ethelred had been so quickly found, responded the jester, and he pointed to the scissors hanging from the tailor's girdle.

reference: ONE NIGHT AS HE LAY IN A DEEP FOREST TOO UNHAPPY TO SLEEP HE HEARD A NOISE NEAR AT HAND IN THE BUSHES
hypothesis: One night as he lay in a deep forest, too unhappy to sleep, he heard a noise near at hand in the bushes.

reference: THOU SHALT HAVE THY LIBERTY HE CRIED EVEN THOUGH THOU SHOULDST REND ME IN PIECES THE MOMENT THOU ART FREE
hypothesis: Thou shalt have thy liberty, he cried, even though thou shouldst rend me in pieces the moment thou art free.

reference: IT HAD SUDDENLY DISAPPEARED AND IN ITS PLACE STOOD A BEAUTIFUL FAIRY WITH FILMY WINGS WHICH SHONE LIKE RAINBOWS IN THE MOONLIGHT
hypothesis: It had suddenly disappeared and in its place stood a beautiful fairy with filmy wings which shone like rainbows in the moonlight.

reference: AT THIS MOMENT THERE WAS A DISTANT RUMBLING AS OF THUNDER TIS THE OGRE CRIED THE FAIRY WE MUST HASTEN
hypothesis: At this moment there was a distant rumbling as of thunder. Tis the ogre, cried the fairy. We must hasten.

reference: SCISSORS GROW A GIANT'S HEIGHT AND SAVE US FROM THE OGRE'S MIGHT
hypothesis: Scissors grow a giant's height and save us from the ogre's might.

reference: HE COULD SEE THE OGRE STANDING POWERLESS TO HURT HIM ON THE OTHER SIDE OF THE CHASM AND GNASHING HIS TEETH EACH ONE OF WHICH WAS AS BIG AS A MILLSTON
hypothesis: He could see the ogre standing powerless to hurt him on the other side of the chasm and gnashing his teeth, each one of which was as big as a millstone.

reference: THE SIGHT WAS SO TERRIBLE THAT HE TURNED ON HIS HEEL AND FLED AWAY AS FAST AS HIS FEET COULD CARRY HIM
hypothesis: The sight was so terrible that he turned on his heel and fled away as fast as his feet could carry him.

reference: THOU SHALT NOT BE LEFT A PRISONER IN THIS DISMAL SPOT WHILE I HAVE THE POWER TO HELP THEE
hypothesis: Thou shalt not be left a prisoner in this dismal spot while I have the power to help thee.

reference: HE LIFTED THE SCISSORS AND WITH ONE STROKE DESTROYED THE WEB AND GAVE THE FLY ITS FREEDOM
hypothesis: He lifted the scissors and with one stroke destroyed the web and gave the fly its freedom.

reference: A FAINT GLIMMER OF LIGHT ON THE OPPOSITE WALL SHOWS ME THE KEYHOLE
hypothesis: A faint glimmer of light on the opposite wall shows me the keyhole.

reference: THE PRINCE SPENT ALL THE FOLLOWING TIME UNTIL MIDNIGHT TRYING TO THINK OF A SUITABLE VERSE TO SAY TO THE SCISSORS
hypothesis: The prince spent all the following time until midnight trying to think of a suitable verse to say to the scissors.

reference: AS HE UTTERED THE WORDS THE SCISSORS LEAPED OUT OF HIS HAND AND BEGAN TO CUT THROUGH THE WOODEN SHUTTERS AS EASILY AS THROUGH A CHEESE
hypothesis: As he uttered the words, the scissors leaped out of his hand and began to cut through the wooden shutters as easily as through a cheese.

reference: IN A VERY SHORT TIME THE PRINCE HAD CRAWLED THROUGH THE OPENING
hypothesis: In a very short time the prince had crawled through the opening.

reference: WHILE HE STOOD LOOKING AROUND HIM IN BEWILDERMENT A FIREFLY ALIGHTED ON HIS ARM FLASHING ITS LITTLE LANTERN IN THE PRINCE'S FACE IT CRIED THIS WAY MY FRIEND THE FLY SENT ME TO GUIDE YOU TO A PLACE OF SAFETY
hypothesis: While he stood looking around him in bewilderment, a firefly alighted on his arm. Flashing its little lantern in the prince's face, it cried

reference: WHAT IS TO BECOME OF ME CRIED THE POOR PEASANT
hypothesis: What is to become of me? cried the poor peasant.

reference: MY GRAIN MUST FALL AND ROT IN THE FIELD FROM OVERRIPENESS BECAUSE I HAVE NOT THE STRENGTH TO RISE AND HARVEST IT THEN INDEED MUST WE ALL STARVE
hypothesis: My grain must fall and rot in the field from over ripeness because I have not the strength to rise and harvest it. Then indeed must we all starve.

hypothesis: The grand dame whom he supplied with faggots, the merchant whom he rescued from robbers, the king's counsellor to whom he gave aid, all became his friends up and down the land. hypothesis: The basis of authority is bodily violence.

reference: THE POSSIBILITY OF APPLYING BODILY VIOLENCE TO PEOPLE IS PROVIDED ABOVE ALL BY AN ORGANIZATION OF ARMED MEN TRAINED TO ACT IN UNISON IN SUBMISSION TO ONE WILL
hypothesis: The possibility of applying bodily violence to people is provided above all by an organization of armed men trained to act in unison and submission to one will.

reference: THESE BANDS OF ARMED MEN SUBMISSIVE TO A SINGLE WILL ARE WHAT CONSTITUTE THE ARMY
hypothesis: These bands of armed men submissive to a single will are what constitute the army.

reference: THE ARMY HAS ALWAYS BEEN AND STILL IS THE BASIS OF POWER
hypothesis: The army has always been and still is the basis of power.

reference: POWER IS ALWAYS IN THE HANDS OF THOSE WHO CONTROL THE ARMY AND ALL MEN IN POWER FROM THE ROMAN CAESARS TO THE RUSSIAN AND GERMAN EMPERORS TAKE MORE INTEREST IN THEIR ARMY THAN IN ANYTHING AND COURT POPULARITY IN THE ARMY KNOWING THAT IF THAT IS ON THEIR SIDE THEIR POWER IS SECURE
hypothesis: Power is always in the hands of those who control the army. and all men in power, from the Roman Caesars to the Russian and German emperors, take more interest in their army than in anything and court popularity in the army, knowing that if that is on their side, their power is secure.

reference: INDEED IT COULD NOT BE OTHERWISE
hypothesis: Indeed, it could not be otherwise.

reference: ONLY UNDER THOSE CONDITIONS COULD THE SOCIAL ORGANIZATION BE JUSTIFIED
hypothesis: Only under those conditions could the social organization be justified.

reference: BUT SINCE THIS IS NOT THE CASE AND ON THE CONTRARY MEN IN POWER ARE ALWAYS FAR FROM BEING SAINTS THROUGH THE VERY FACT OF THEIR POSSESSION OF POWER THE SOCIAL ORGANIZATION BASED ON POWER HAS NO JUSTIFICATION
hypothesis: But since this is not the case, and on the contrary, men in power are always far from being saints through the very fact of their possession of power, the social organization based on power has no justification.

reference: EVEN IF THERE WAS ONCE A TIME WHEN OWING TO THE LOW STANDARD OF MORALS AND THE DISPOSITION OF MEN TO VIOLENCE THE EXISTENCE OF AN AUTHORITY TO RESTRAIN SUCH VIOLENCE WAS AN ADVANTAGE BECAUSE THE VIOLENCE OF GOVERNMENT WAS LESS THAN THE VIOLENCE OF INDIVIDUALS ONE CANNOT BUT SEE THAT THIS ADVANTAGE COULD NOT BE LASTING
hypothesis: Even if there was once a time when, owing to the low standards of morals and the disposition of men to violence, the existence of an authority to restrain such violence was an advantage because the violence of the government was less than the violence of individuals, one cannot but see that this advantage could not be lasting.

reference: BETWEEN THE MEMBERS OF ONE STATE SUBJECT TO A SINGLE AUTHORITY THE STRIFE BETWEEN INDIVIDUALS SEEMS STILL LESS AND THE LIFE OF THE STATE SEEMS EVEN MORE SECURE
hypothesis: Between the members of one state subject to a single authority, the strife between the individuals seems still less and the life of the state seems even more secure.

reference: IT WAS PRODUCED ON ONE HAND BY THE NATURAL GROWTH OF POPULATION AND ON THE OTHER BY STRUGGLE AND CONQUEST
hypothesis: It was produced on one hand by the natural growth of population and on the other by struggle and conquest.

reference: AFTER CONQUEST THE POWER OF THE EMPEROR PUTS AN END TO INTERNAL DISSENSIONS AND SO THE STATE CONCEPTION OF LIFE JUSTIFIES ITSELF
hypothesis: After conquest, the power of the emperor puts an end to internal dissensions. and so the state conception of life justifies itself.

reference: BUT THIS JUSTIFICATION IS NEVER MORE THAN TEMPORARY
hypothesis: But this justification is never more than temporary.

reference: INTERNAL DISSENSIONS DISAPPEAR ONLY IN PROPORTION TO THE DEGREE OF OPPRESSION EXERTED BY THE AUTHORITY OVER THE DISSENTIENT INDIVIDUALS
hypothesis: Internal dissensions disappear only in proportion to the degree of oppression exerted by the authority over the dissentient individuals.

reference: GOVERNMENT AUTHORITY EVEN IF IT DOES SUPPRESS PRIVATE VIOLENCE ALWAYS INTRODUCES INTO THE LIFE OF MEN FRESH FORMS OF VIOLENCE WHICH TEND TO BECOME GREATER AND GREATER IN PROPORTION TO THE DURATION AND STRENGTH OF THE GOVERNMENT
hypothesis: Government authority, even if it does suppress private violence, always introduces into the life of men fresh forms of violence which tend to become greater and greater in proportion to the duration and strength of the government.

reference: AND THEREFORE THE OPPRESSION OF THE OPPRESSED ALWAYS GOES ON GROWING UP TO THE FURTHEST LIMIT BEYOND WHICH IT CANNOT GO WITHOUT KILLING THE GOOSE WITH THE GOLDEN EGGS
hypothesis: And therefore the oppression of the oppressed always goes on growing up to the furthest limit beyond which it cannot go without killing the goose with the golden eggs.

reference: THE MOST CONVINCING EXAMPLE OF THIS IS TO BE FOUND IN THE CONDITION OF THE WORKING CLASSES OF OUR EPOCH WHO ARE IN REALITY NO BETTER THAN THE SLAVES OF ANCIENT TIMES SUBDUED BY CONQUEST
hypothesis: The most convincing example of this is to be found in the condition of the working classes of our epoch, who are in reality no better than the slaves of ancient times subdued by conquest.

reference: SO IT HAS ALWAYS BEEN
hypothesis: So it has always been.

reference: FOOTNOTE THE FACT THAT IN AMERICA THE ABUSES OF AUTHORITY EXIST IN SPITE OF THE SMALL NUMBER OF THEIR TROOPS NOT ONLY FAILS TO DISPROVE THIS POSITION BUT POSITIVELY CONFIRMS IT
hypothesis: Footnote

reference: THE UPPER CLASSES KNOW THAT AN ARMY OF FIFTY THOUSAND WILL SOON BE INSUFFICIENT AND NO LONGER RELYING ON PINKERTON'S MEN THEY FEEL THAT THE SECURITY OF THEIR POSITION DEPENDS ON THE INCREASED STRENGTH OF THE ARMY
hypothesis: The upper classes know that an army of 50000 will soon be insufficient and no longer relying on Pinkerton's men, they feel that the security of their position depends on the increased strength of the army.

reference: THE REASON TO WHICH HE GAVE EXPRESSION IS ESSENTIALLY THE SAME AS THAT WHICH MADE THE FRENCH KINGS AND THE POPES ENGAGE SWISS AND SCOTCH GUARDS AND MAKES THE RUSSIAN AUTHORITIES OF TO DAY SO CAREFULLY DISTRIBUTE THE RECRUITS SO THAT THE REGIMENTS FROM THE FRONTIERS ARE STATIONED IN CENTRAL DISTRICTS AND THE REGIMENTS FROM THE CENTER ARE STATIONED ON THE FRONTIERS
hypothesis: The reason to which he gave expression is essentially the same as that which made the French kings and the popes engage Swiss and Scotch guards and makes the Russian authorities of today so carefully distribute the recruits so that the regiments from the frontiers are stationed in central districts and the regiments from the center are stationed on the frontiers.

reference: THE MEANING OF CAPRIVI'S SPEECH PUT INTO PLAIN LANGUAGE IS THAT FUNDS ARE NEEDED NOT TO RESIST FOREIGN FOES BUT TO BUY UNDER OFFICERS TO BE READY TO ACT AGAINST THE ENSLAVED TOILING MASSES
hypothesis: The meaning of Capri speech put into plain language is that funds are needed not to resist foreign foes, but to buy under officers to be ready to act against the enslaved, toiling masses.

reference: AND THIS ABNORMAL ORDER OF THINGS IS MAINTAINED BY THE ARMY
hypothesis: And this abnormal order of things is maintained by the army.

reference: BUT THERE IS NOT ONLY ONE GOVERNMENT THERE ARE OTHER GOVERNMENTS EXPLOITING THEIR SUBJECTS BY VIOLENCE IN THE SAME WAY AND ALWAYS READY TO POUNCE DOWN ON ANY OTHER GOVERNMENT AND CARRY OFF THE FRUITS OF THE TOIL OF ITS ENSLAVED SUBJECTS
hypothesis: But there is not only one government, there are other governments exploiting their subjects by violence in the same way and are always ready to pounce down on any other government and carry off the fruits of the toil of its enslaved subjects.

reference: AND SO EVERY GOVERNMENT NEEDS AN ARMY ALSO TO PROTECT ITS BOOTY FROM ITS NEIGHBOR BRIGANDS
hypothesis: And so every government needs an army also to protect its booty from its neighbor brigands.

reference: THIS INCREASE IS CONTAGIOUS AS MONTESQUIEU POINTED OUT ONE HUNDRED FIFTY YEARS AGO
hypothesis: This increase is contagious, as Montesquieu pointed out 150 years ago.

reference: EVERY INCREASE IN THE ARMY OF ONE STATE WITH THE AIM OF SELF DEFENSE AGAINST ITS SUBJECTS BECOMES A SOURCE OF DANGER FOR NEIGHBORING STATES AND CALLS FOR A SIMILAR INCREASE IN THEIR ARMIES
hypothesis: Every increase in the army of one state with the aim of self defense against its subjects becomes a source of danger for neighboring states and calls for a similar increase in their armies.

reference: THE DESPOTISM OF A GOVERNMENT ALWAYS INCREASES WITH THE STRENGTH OF THE ARMY AND ITS EXTERNAL SUCCESSES AND THE AGGRESSIVENESS OF A GOVERNMENT INCREASES WITH ITS INTERNAL DESPOTISM
hypothesis: The despotism of the government always increases with the strength of the army and its external successes, and the aggressiveness of a government increases with its internal despotism.

reference: THE RIVALRY OF THE EUROPEAN STATES IN CONSTANTLY INCREASING THEIR FORCES HAS REDUCED THEM TO THE NECESSITY OF HAVING RECOURSE TO UNIVERSAL MILITARY SERVICE SINCE BY THAT MEANS THE GREATEST POSSIBLE NUMBER OF SOLDIERS IS OBTAINED AT THE LEAST POSSIBLE EXPENSE
hypothesis: The rivalry of the European states in constantly increasing their forces has reduced them to the necessity of having recourse to universal military service.

reference: AND BY THIS MEANS ALL CITIZENS ARE UNDER ARMS TO SUPPORT THE INIQUITIES PRACTICED UPON THEM ALL CITIZENS HAVE BECOME THEIR OWN OPPRESSORS
hypothesis: And by this means all citizens are under arms to support the iniquities practiced upon them. All citizens have become their own oppressors.

reference: THIS INCONSISTENCY HAS BECOME OBVIOUS IN UNIVERSAL MILITARY SERVICE
hypothesis: This inconsistency has become obvious in universal military service.

reference: IN FACT THE WHOLE SIGNIFICANCE OF THE SOCIAL CONCEPTION OF LIFE CONSISTS IN MAN'S RECOGNITION OF THE BARBARITY OF STRIFE BETWEEN INDIVIDUALS AND THE TRANSITORINESS OF PERSONAL LIFE ITSELF AND THE TRANSFERENCE OF THE AIM OF LIFE TO GROUPS OF PERSONS
hypothesis: In fact, the whole significance of the social conception of life consists in man's recognition of the barbarity of strife between individuals and the transits of personal life itself and the transference of the aim of life to groups of persons.

reference: BUT WITH UNIVERSAL MILITARY SERVICE IT COMES TO PASS THAT MEN AFTER MAKING EVERY SACRIFICE TO GET RID OF THE CRUELTY OF STRIFE AND THE INSECURITY OF EXISTENCE ARE CALLED UPON TO FACE ALL THE PERILS THEY HAD MEANT TO AVOID
hypothesis: But with universal military service, it comes to pass that men, after making every sacrifice to get rid of the cruelty of strife and the insecurity of existence, are called upon to face all the perils they had meant to avoid.

reference: BUT INSTEAD OF DOING THAT THEY EXPOSE THE INDIVIDUALS TO THE SAME NECESSITY OF STRIFE SUBSTITUTING STRIFE WITH INDIVIDUALS OF OTHER STATES FOR STRIFE WITH NEIGHBORS
hypothesis: But instead of doing that, they expose the individuals to the same necessity of strife, substituting strife with individuals of other states for strife with neighbors.

reference: THE TAXES RAISED FROM THE PEOPLE FOR WAR PREPARATIONS ABSORB THE GREATER PART OF THE PRODUCE OF LABOR WHICH THE ARMY OUGHT TO DEFEND
hypothesis: The taxes raised from the people for war preparations absorb the greater part of the produce of labor which the army ought to defend.

reference: THE DANGER OF WAR EVER READY TO BREAK OUT RENDERS ALL REFORMS OF LIFE SOCIAL LIFE VAIN AND FRUITLESS
hypothesis: The danger of war ever ready to break out renders all reforms of life, social life, vain and fruitless.

reference: BUT THE FATAL SIGNIFICANCE OF UNIVERSAL MILITARY SERVICE AS THE MANIFESTATION OF THE CONTRADICTION INHERENT IN THE SOCIAL CONCEPTION OF LIFE IS NOT ONLY APPARENT IN THAT
hypothesis: But the fatal significance of universal military service as the manifestation of the contradiction inherent in the social conception of life is not only apparent in that.

reference: GOVERNMENTS ASSERT THAT ARMIES ARE NEEDED ABOVE ALL FOR EXTERNAL DEFENSE BUT THAT IS NOT TRUE
hypothesis: Governments assert that armies are needed above all for external defense, but that is not true.

reference: THEY ARE NEEDED PRINCIPALLY AGAINST THEIR SUBJECTS AND EVERY MAN UNDER UNIVERSAL MILITARY SERVICE BECOMES AN ACCOMPLICE IN ALL THE ACTS OF VIOLENCE OF THE GOVERNMENT AGAINST THE CITIZENS WITHOUT ANY CHOICE OF HIS OWN
hypothesis: They are needed principally against their subjects. and every man under universal military service becomes an accomplice in all the acts of violence of the government against the citizens without any choice of his own.

reference: AND FOR THE SAKE OF WHAT AM I MAKING THEM
hypothesis: And for the sake of what am I making them?

reference: I AM EXPECTED FOR THE SAKE OF THE STATE TO MAKE THESE SACRIFICES TO RENOUNCE EVERYTHING THAT CAN BE PRECIOUS TO MAN PEACE FAMILY SECURITY AND HUMAN DIGNITY
hypothesis: I am expected for the sake of the state to make these sacrifices, to renounce everything that can be precious to man, peace, family, security and human dignity.

reference: EXCEPT FOR THE STATE THEY SAY WE SHOULD BE EXPOSED TO THE ATTACKS OF EVIL DISPOSED PERSONS IN OUR OWN COUNTRY
hypothesis: Except for the state, they say we should be exposed to the attacks of evil disposed persons in our own country.

reference: WE KNOW NOW THAT THREATS AND PUNISHMENTS CANNOT DIMINISH THEIR NUMBER THAT THAT CAN ONLY BE DONE BY CHANGE OF ENVIRONMENT AND MORAL INFLUENCE
hypothesis: We now know that threats and punishments cannot diminish their number, but that can only be done by change of environment and moral influence.

reference: SO THAT THE JUSTIFICATION OF STATE VIOLENCE ON THE GROUND OF THE PROTECTION IT GIVES US FROM EVIL DISPOSED PERSONS EVEN IF IT HAD SOME FOUNDATION THREE OR FOUR CENTURIES AGO HAS NONE WHATEVER NOW
hypothesis: So that the justification of state violence on the ground of the protection it gives us from evil disposed persons, even if it had some foundation three or four centuries ago, has none whatever now.

reference: EXCEPT FOR THE STATE THEY TELL US WE SHOULD NOT HAVE ANY RELIGION EDUCATION CULTURE MEANS OF COMMUNICATION AND SO ON
hypothesis: Except for the state, they tell us we should not have any religion, education, culture, means of communication and so on.

reference: WITHOUT THE STATE MEN WOULD NOT HAVE BEEN ABLE TO FORM THE SOCIAL INSTITUTIONS NEEDED FOR DOING ANY THING
hypothesis: Without the state, men would not have been able to form the social institutions needed for doing anything.

reference: THIS ARGUMENT TOO WAS WELL FOUNDED ONLY SOME CENTURIES AGO
hypothesis: This argument too was well founded only some centuries ago.

reference: THE GREAT EXTENSION OF MEANS OF COMMUNICATION AND INTERCHANGE OF IDEAS HAS MADE MEN COMPLETELY ABLE TO DISPENSE WITH STATE AID IN FORMING SOCIETIES ASSOCIATIONS CORPORATIONS AND CONGRESSES FOR SCIENTIFIC ECONOMIC AND POLITICAL OBJECTS
hypothesis: The great extension of means of communication and interchange of ideas has made men completely able to dispense with state aid in forming societies, associations, corporations and congresses for scientific, economic and political objects.

reference: WITHOUT GOVERNMENTS NATIONS WOULD BE ENSLAVED BY THEIR NEIGHBORS
hypothesis: Without governments, nations would be enslaved by their neighbors.

reference: THE GOVERNMENT THEY TELL US WITH ITS ARMY IS NECESSARY TO DEFEND US FROM NEIGHBORING STATES WHO MIGHT ENSLAVE US
hypothesis: The government, they tell us, with its army is necessary to defend us from neighboring states who might enslave us.

reference: AND IF DEFENSE AGAINST BARBAROUS NATIONS IS MEANT ONE THOUSANDTH PART OF THE TROOPS NOW UNDER ARMS WOULD BE AMPLY SUFFICIENT FOR THAT PURPOSE
hypothesis: And if defence against barbarous nations is meant, 1000th part of the troops now under arms would be amply sufficient for that purpose.

reference: THE POWER OF THE STATE FAR FROM BEING A SECURITY AGAINST THE ATTACKS OF OUR NEIGHBORS EXPOSES US ON THE CONTRARY TO MUCH GREATER DANGER OF SUCH ATTACKS
hypothesis: The power of the State, far from being a security against the attacks of our neighbors, exposes us, on the contrary, to much greater danger of such attacks.

reference: EVEN LOOKING AT IT PRACTICALLY WEIGHING THAT IS TO SAY ALL THE BURDENS LAID ON HIM BY THE STATE NO MAN CAN FAIL TO SEE THAT FOR HIM PERSONALLY TO COMPLY WITH STATE DEMANDS AND SERVE IN THE ARMY WOULD IN THE MAJORITY OF CASES BE MORE DISADVANTAGEOUS THAN TO REFUSE TO DO SO
hypothesis: Even looking at it, practically weighing, that is to say, all the burdens laid on him by the state.

reference: TO RESIST WOULD NEED INDEPENDENT THOUGHT AND EFFORT OF WHICH EVERY MAN IS NOT CAPABLE
hypothesis: To resist would need independent thought and effort of which every man is not capable.

reference: SO MUCH FOR THE ADVANTAGES AND DISADVANTAGES OF BOTH LINES OF CONDUCT FOR A MAN OF THE WEALTHY CLASSES AN OPPRESSOR
hypothesis: So much for the advantages and disadvantages of both lines of conduct. For a man of the wealthy class, an oppressor.

reference: FOR A MAN OF THE POOR WORKING CLASS THE ADVANTAGES AND DISADVANTAGES WILL BE THE SAME BUT WITH A GREAT INCREASE OF DISADVANTAGES
hypothesis: For a man of the poor working class, the advantages and disadvantages will be the same, but with a great increase of disadvantages.

reference: THE COMMONS ALSO VOTED THAT THE NEW CREATED PEERS OUGHT TO HAVE NO VOICE IN THIS TRIAL BECAUSE THE ACCUSATION BEING AGREED TO WHILE THEY WERE COMMONERS THEIR CONSENT TO IT WAS IMPLIED WITH THAT OF ALL THE COMMONS OF ENGLAND
hypothesis: The Commons also voted that the new created peers ought to have no voice in this trial, because the accusation being agreed to while they were commoners, their consent to it was implied with that of all the Commons of England.

reference: IN THE GOVERNMENT OF IRELAND HIS ADMINISTRATION HAD BEEN EQUALLY PROMOTIVE OF HIS MASTER'S INTEREST AND THAT OF THE SUBJECTS COMMITTED TO HIS CARE
hypothesis: In the government of Ireland, his administration had been equally promo of his master's interest and that of the subjects committed to his care.

reference: THE CASE OF LORD MOUNTNORRIS OF ALL THOSE WHICH WERE COLLECTED WITH SO MUCH INDUSTRY IS THE MOST FLAGRANT AND THE LEAST EXCUSABLE
hypothesis: The case of Lord Mont Norris, of all those which were collected with so much industry, is the most flagrant and the least excusable.

reference: THE COURT WHICH CONSISTED OF THE CHIEF OFFICERS OF THE ARMY FOUND THE CRIME TO BE CAPITAL AND CONDEMNED THAT NOBLEMAN TO LOSE HIS HEAD
hypothesis: The court, which consisted of the chief officials of the army, found the crime to be capital and condemned that nobleman to lose his head.

reference: WHERE THE TOKEN BY WHICH I SHOULD DISCOVER IT
hypothesis: Where the token by which I shall discover it.

reference: IT IS NOW FULL TWO HUNDRED AND FORTY YEARS SINCE TREASONS WERE DEFINED AND SO LONG HAS IT BEEN SINCE ANY MAN WAS TOUCHED TO THIS EXTENT UPON THIS CRIME BEFORE MYSELF
hypothesis: It is now a full 240 years since treasons were defined and so long has it been since any man was touched to this extent upon this crime. For myself.

reference: LET US NOT TO OUR OWN DESTRUCTION AWAKE THOSE SLEEPING LIONS BY RATTLING UP A COMPANY OF OLD RECORDS WHICH HAVE LAIN FOR SO MANY AGES BY THE WALL FORGOTTEN AND NEGLECTED
hypothesis: Let us not to our own destruction awake those sleeping lions by rattling up a company of old records which had lain for so many ages by the wall, forgotten and neglected.

reference: HOWEVER THESE GENTLEMEN AT THE BAR SAY THEY SPEAK FOR THE COMMONWEALTH AND THEY BELIEVE SO YET UNDER FAVOR IT IS I WHO IN THIS PARTICULAR SPEAK FOR THE COMMONWEALTH
hypothesis: However, these gentlemen at the bar say they speak for the Commonwealth and they believe so.

reference: MY LORDS I HAVE NOW TROUBLED YOUR LORDSHIPS A GREAT DEAL LONGER THAN I SHOULD HAVE DONE
hypothesis: My Lords, I have now troubled your Lordships a great deal longer than I should have done.

reference: YOUNG VANE FALLING UPON THIS PAPER OF NOTES DEEMED THE MATTER OF THE UTMOST IMPORTANCE AND IMMEDIATELY COMMUNICATED IT TO PYM WHO NOW PRODUCED THE PAPER BEFORE THE HOUSE OF COMMONS
hypothesis: Young Vane, falling upon this paper of notes, deemed the matter of the utmost importance, and immediately communicated it to Pym, who now produced the paper before the House of Commons.

reference: THE KING PROPOSES THIS DIFFICULTY BUT HOW CAN I UNDERTAKE OFFENSIVE WAR IF I HAVE NO MORE MONEY
hypothesis: The King proposes this difficulty, but how can I undertake offensive war if I have no more money?

reference: YOUR MAJESTY HAVING TRIED THE AFFECTIONS OF YOUR PEOPLE YOU ARE ABSOLVED AND LOOSE FROM ALL RULES OF GOVERNMENT AND MAY DO WHAT POWER WILL ADMIT
hypothesis: Your Majesty, having tried the affections of your people, you are absolved and loose from all rules of government.

reference: THE GUILD OF SAINT ELIZABETH
hypothesis: The Guild of St.. Elizabeth.

reference: IMMEDIATELY AFTER DINNER THAT EVENING LESLIE RAN UP TO HER ROOM TO MAKE PREPARATIONS FOR HER VISIT TO EAST HALL
hypothesis: Immediately after dinner that evening, Leslie ran up to her room to make preparations for her visit to East Hall.

reference: I'M NOT COMING SAID ANNIE
hypothesis: I'm not coming, said Annie.

reference: EVERY STUDENT IS TO BE IN EAST HALL AT HALF PAST EIGHT
hypothesis: Every student is to be in East Hall at 08:30.

reference: IT DOESN'T MATTER REPLIED ANNIE WHETHER IT IS AN ORDER OR NOT I'M NOT COMING SAY NOTHING ABOUT ME PLEASE
hypothesis: It doesn't matter, replied Annie, whether it is in an order or not. I'm not coming. Say nothing about me, please.

reference: IT BURNED AS IF WITH FEVER
hypothesis: It burned as if with fever.

reference: YOU DON'T KNOW WHAT A TRIAL IT IS FOR ME TO HAVE YOU HERE
hypothesis: You don't know what a trial it is for me to have you here.

reference: I WANT TO BE ALONE GO
hypothesis: I want to be alone. Go.

reference: I KNOW YOU DON'T QUITE MEAN WHAT YOU SAY SAID LESLIE BUT OF COURSE IF YOU REALLY WISH ME
hypothesis: I know you don't quite mean what you say, said Leslie, but of course if you really wish me.

reference: YOU FRET ME BEYOND ENDURANCE
hypothesis: You fret me beyond endurance.

reference: WRAPPING A PRETTY BLUE SHAWL ROUND HER HEAD AND SHOULDERS SHE TURNED TO ANNIE
hypothesis: Wrapping a pretty blue shawl around her head and shoulders, she turned to Annie.

reference: LESLIE WAS JUST CLOSING THE DOOR BEHIND HER WHEN ANNIE CALLED AFTER HER
hypothesis: Leslie was just closing the door behind her when Annie called after her.

reference: I TOOK IT OUT SAID LESLIE TOOK IT OUT
hypothesis: I took it out, said Lily, took it out.

reference: HAVE THE GOODNESS TO FIND IT AND PUT IT BACK
hypothesis: Have the goodness to find it and put it back.

reference: BUT DON'T LOCK ME OUT PLEASE ANNIE
hypothesis: But don't lock me out, please, Annie.

reference: OH I WON'T LOCK YOU OUT SHE SAID BUT I MUST HAVE THE KEY
hypothesis: Oh, I won't lock you out, she said, but I must have the key.

reference: JANE HERIOT'S VOICE WAS HEARD IN THE PASSAGE
hypothesis: Jane Harriet's voice was heard in the passage.

reference: AS SHE WALKED DOWN THE CORRIDOR SHE HEARD IT BEING TURNED IN THE LOCK
hypothesis: As she walked down the corridor, she heard it being turned in the lock.

reference: WHAT CAN THIS MEAN SHE SAID TO HERSELF
hypothesis: What can this mean? she said to herself.

reference: OH I WON'T PRESS YOU REPLIED JANE
hypothesis: Oh, I won't press you, replied Jane.

reference: OH I SHALL NEVER DO THAT REPLIED LESLIE
hypothesis: Oh, I shall never do that, replied Leslie.

reference: YOU SEE ALL THE GIRLS EXCEPT EILEEN AND MARJORIE LAUGH AT HER AND THAT SEEMS TO ME TO MAKE HER WORSE
hypothesis: You see, all the girls except Eileen and Marjorie laugh at her and that seems to me to make her worse.

reference: SOME DAY JANE YOU MUST SEE HER
hypothesis: Some day, Jane, you must see her.

reference: IF YOU ARE IN LONDON DURING THE SUMMER YOU MUST COME AND PAY US A VISIT WILL YOU
hypothesis: If you are in London during the summer, you must come and pay us a visit. will you?

reference: THAT IS IF YOU CARE TO CONFIDE IN ME
hypothesis: That is, if you care to pardon me.

reference: I BELIEVE POOR ANNIE IS DREADFULLY UNHAPPY
hypothesis: I believe poor Annie is dreadfully unhappy.

reference: THAT'S JUST IT JANE THAT IS WHAT FRIGHTENS ME SHE REFUSES TO COME
hypothesis: That's just it, Jane. That is what frightens me. She refuses to come.

reference: REFUSES TO COME SHE CRIED
hypothesis: Refuses to come, she cried.

reference: SHE WILL GET INTO AN AWFUL SCRAPE
hypothesis: She'll get in an awful scrape.

reference: I AM SURE SHE IS ILL SHE WORKS TOO HARD AND SHE BUT THERE I DON'T KNOW THAT I OUGHT TO SAY ANY MORE
hypothesis: I am sure she is ill. she works too hard and she. but there, I don't know that I ought to say any more.

reference: I'LL WAIT FOR YOU HERE SAID LESLIE
hypothesis: I'll wait for you here, said Leslie.

reference: DO COME ANNIE DO
hypothesis: Do come, Annie, do.

reference: SCARCELY LIKELY REPLIED LESLIE SHE TOLD ME SHE WAS DETERMINED NOT TO COME TO THE MEETING
hypothesis: Scarcely likely, replied Leslie. She told me she was determined not to come to the meeting.

reference: BUT MARJORIE AND EILEEN HAD ALREADY DEPARTED AND LESLIE AND JANE FOUND THEMSELVES AMONG THE LAST STUDENTS TO ARRIVE AT THE GREAT EAST HALL
hypothesis: But Marjorie and Eileen had already departed, and Leslie and Jane found themselves among the last students to arrive at the Great East Hall.

reference: MISS LAUDERDALE WAS STANDING WITH THE OTHER TUTORS AND PRINCIPALS OF THE DIFFERENT HALLS ON A RAISED PLATFORM
hypothesis: Miss Lauderdale was standing with the other tutors and principals of the different halls on a raised platform.

reference: THEN A ROLL CALL WAS GONE THROUGH BY ONE OF THE TUTORS THE ONLY ABSENTEE WAS ANNIE COLCHESTER
hypothesis: Then a roll call was gone through by one of the tutors. The only absentee was Annie Colchester.

reference: THE PHYSICAL PART OF YOUR TRAINING AND ALSO THE MENTAL PART ARE ABUNDANTLY SUPPLIED IN THIS GREAT HOUSE OF LEARNING SHE CONTINUED BUT THE SPIRITUAL PART IT SEEMS TO ME OUGHT NOW TO BE STRENGTHENED
hypothesis: The physical part of your training and also the mental part are abundantly supplied in this great house of learning, she continued. But the spiritual part, it seems to me, ought now to be strengthened.

reference: HEAR HEAR AND ONCE AGAIN HEAR
hypothesis: Ha ha. And once again here.

reference: SHE UTTERED HER STRANGE REMARK STANDING UP
hypothesis: She uttered her stream remark standing up.

reference: MARJORIE AND EILEEN WERE CLOSE TO HER
hypothesis: Marjorie and Eileen were close to her.

reference: I WILL TALK WITH YOU BELLE ACHESON PRESENTLY SHE SAID
hypothesis: I will talk with you, Belle Archon, presently, she said.

reference: THE NAMES OF PROPOSED MEMBERS ARE TO BE SUBMITTED TO ME BEFORE THIS DAY WEEK
hypothesis: The names of the proposed members are to be submitted to me before this day week.

reference: AM I MY BROTHER'S KEEPER
hypothesis: Am I my brother's keeper?

reference: YOU ASK SHE CONTINUED
hypothesis: You ask, she continued,

reference: GOD ANSWERS TO EACH OF YOU YOU ARE
hypothesis: God answers each of you. You are.

reference: THE WORLD SAYS NO I AM NOT BUT GOD SAYS YES YOU ARE
hypothesis: The world says, no, I am not, but God says, yes, you are.

reference: ALL MEN ARE YOUR BROTHERS
hypothesis: All men are your brothers.

reference: FOR ALL WHO SIN ALL WHO SUFFER YOU ARE TO A CERTAIN EXTENT RESPONSIBLE
hypothesis: For all who sin, all who suffer, you are to a certain extent responsible.

reference: AFTER THE ADDRESS THE GIRLS THEMSELVES WERE ENCOURAGED TO SPEAK AND A VERY ANIMATED DISCUSSION FOLLOWED
hypothesis: After the address, the girls themselves were encouraged to speak and a very animated discussion followed.

reference: IT WAS PAST TEN O'CLOCK WHEN SHE LEFT THE HALL
hypothesis: It was past 10:00 when she left the hall.

reference: JUST AS SHE WAS DOING SO MISS FRERE CAME UP
hypothesis: Just as she was doing so, Miss Frere came up.

reference: ANNIE COLCHESTER IS YOUR ROOMFELLOW IS SHE NOT SHE SAID
hypothesis: Annie Colchester is your room fellow, is she not? she said.

reference: I SEE BY YOUR FACE MISS GILROY THAT YOU ARE DISTRESSED ABOUT SOMETHING ARE YOU KEEPING ANYTHING BACK
hypothesis: I see by your face, Miss Gilroy, that you are distressed about something. Are you keeping anything back?

reference: I AM AFRAID I AM REPLIED LESLIE DISTRESS NOW IN HER TONE
hypothesis: I'm afraid I am, replied Leslie, distress now in her tone.
